segments.s307-g393: ز هر سو is a prepositional/locative phrase 'from every side' (ز = 'from', here poetic). فرستادگان is the noun 'envoys' and is followed by نزد شاه, which gives their destination/purpose ('toward/in the presence of the king'). Word order is topical: the source ('ز هر سو') and the travelers ('فرستادگان') are presented before the phrase indicating their destination ('نزد شاه').
segments.s307-g394: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' modifying the following verbal phrase. سر اندر نهاده is a compact, somewhat idiomatic participial construction: سر ('head') + اندر ('into/in') + نهاده (past participle 'having put/placed') — literally 'having put (their) head into', idiomatically 'set out' or 'started off'. به راه ('on/to the road') gives the path/means. The clause lacks an explicit finite auxiliary (typical in epic/poetic style), so the participle conveys the past action.
segments.s307-g395: ابا here functions as a prepositional marker equivalent to با ('with') in this poetic context, introducing what accompanies the action. هدیه و نامه lists the accompanying items ('gifts and letters'). مهتران ('the chiefs/elder nobles') is the source/owners of those gifts and letters; the final word is a plural noun meaning the great/important persons (an older poetic term).
segments.s307-g396: شده is the past participle 'become' (here functioning as the main past action; the subject is omitted and understood from the previous sentence: the envoys). یک به یک ('one by one') is an adverbial phrase modifying شده. شاه را is marked with را: in this construction را marks the person to whom the new relation applies ('the king' as the one to whom they are attendants). چاکران is the predicate noun 'attendants/servants'; together شاه را چاکران شده means 'became (the) king's attendants.'
segments.s307-g297: ز بهر is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning "because of/for the sake of." It governs the following name phrase. که introduces a relative clause modifying سیاوش: "who is in my خان," with در (in) before the place noun and the possessor من following the noun (خانِ من = my khan). This word order (demonstrative/relative after the head) is typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s307-g298: چه here is exclamatory ("what/what a ..."). تیمار بد = "bad/cruel treatment" (noun + adjective; adjective follows noun). بر is the preposition "on/upon" governing the coordinated object دل و جان من ("my heart and life/soul"); the possessive من at the end covers both coordinated nouns.
segments.s307-g299: که at the start introduces the clause identifying the agent. The sequence افراسیاب آن بداندیش مرد is a slightly inverted, emphatic description: افراسیاب (the name) + آن (that/emphatic) + بداندیش مرد ("ill‑minded man"). The demonstrative آن emphasizes or points back to him in a poetic register.
segments.s307-g300: بسی here functions adverbially to mean "much/very many" (poetic usage). پند بشنید = "heard advice/admonitions" (بشنید is simple past ‘he heard’). سودش نکرد is a negated clause meaning "it profited him not / it did him no good," where سودش (sud+pronominal suffix) is the object "benefit to him" and نکرد is past negative of کرد.
segments.s307-g301: بدان is the imperative/hortative sense formed from the prefix ب- + دان ('be‑know' → 'know/consider'), functioning as 'know this' or 'mark well that'. تا introduces a temporal clause ('when/until'), followed by چنین روزش آید (چنین 'such', روزش 'his day' with the possessive suffix -ش, آید 'comes'); آید به سر is the idiom 'come to an end' (آید + prepositional phrase به سر).
segments.s307-g302: شود is the simple present/future sense of شدن here ('will become' / 'will be'), followed by پادشاهیش ('his kingship/sovereignty', noun + possessive -ش). زیر و زبر is a fixed paired expression meaning 'under and over' → 'topsy‑turvy; overturned'; the verb + noun + idiomatic phrase yields 'his rule will be overturned.'
segments.s307-g303: به تاراج داده is a passive‑style verbal phrase: به تاراج ('to plunder/to pillage') + داده (past participle 'given'), meaning 'given over to plunder' or 'plundered'. The direct objects (or items thus plundered) follow: کلاه و کمر ('hat and belt/girdle'), coordinated by و.
segments.s307-g304: شده is the perfect/passive participle ('has become' / 'has been made'), with روز او ('his day' — روز + possessive -او) as the affected noun. تار is a short adjective/descriptor paired with برگشته سر ('and the head reversed/turned back'); برگشته is past participle/adjectival 'returned/turned back' modifying سر ('head'). The clause is compact and poetically elliptical: verb particle + nominal phrase + paired adjectival elements.
segments.s307-g257: ز + ایوان introduces the source/place ('from the iwan'); به زاری is an instrumental/attitudinal phrase 'with lamentation' (به here marks manner); برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth' and خروش is the nominal result 'a cry/uproar' — standard SOV elements collapsed into a compact poetic order.
segments.s307-g258: که‌ای is an archaic vocative/introductory particle (equivalent to که ای) addressing someone; دادگر شاه is the addressee 'just king' (دادگر used as a title); بسیار modifies هوش and here means 'very'; هوش is used in the sense 'awareness/heed' — the whole segment is direct speech: an appeal to the king to be attentive.
segments.s307-g259: تو دانی que-clause: تو (you) + دانی (present 'you know') introduces the subordinate content clause begun by که 'that'; ما سخت بیچاره‌ایم is a present copular clause 'we are severely wretched' where سخت intensifies بیچاره‌ایم.
segments.s307-g260: نه negates the following predicate; بر جای is a prepositional/locative phrase 'in the place/role of'; خواری و پیغاره‌ایم is a coordinated predicate with the final copula -ایم ('we are') applying to both nouns — literally 'we are not in the place of humiliation and (we are not) a notice/warning,' i.e. 'not destined for humiliation or to serve as a lesson.'
segments.s307-g261: بر شاه شد places the verb phrase with an initial directional/prepositional phrase ('before/at the king'), where شد (past) indicates that something 'became/was set' or 'came to be' in that position; مهتر بانوان is the subject 'the chief/elder of the ladies' — a compact, slightly inverted poetic word order (prepositional phrase fronted).
segments.s307-g262: ابا here denotes reluctance ('with refusal/reluctance'); دختران is the noun 'the maidens/daughters'; اندر آمد is a two-word verbal phrase 'entered/came in' (اندر = in/into, آمد = came); نوان is a descriptive epithet 'youthful/new' qualifying the maidens — the whole segment describes the young maidens entering, albeit reluctantly.
segments.s307-g263: پرستنده صد پیش هر دختری is a quantificational noun phrase 'a hundred admirers before each maiden' — پرستنده functions as 'admirer/devotee', صد ('one hundred') quantifies it, and پیش هر دختری ('in front of each girl') gives the spatial relation; Persian permits this compact ordering in poetry.
segments.s307-g264: ز یاقوت بر هر سری افسری is a locative/descrptive clause: ز یاقوت ('of/from ruby' = made of ruby) modifies افسری (a diadem), and بر هر سری ('on every head') gives its position; the verb 'was' is omitted (typical poetic ellipsis), so the sense is 'a ruby diadem (was) on every head.'
segments.s307-g41: دو صد چرخ is a numeral phrase 'two hundred wheels/wheeled (things)' with the numeral before the noun (typical Persian order). بر هر دری is a prepositional phrase ('بر' + 'هر' + دری) meaning 'at/on each gate' — here بر marks location relative to the gate. با کمان is an instrumental/with-phrase ('with bows'), modifying the wheeled engines as equipped with bows or archers.
segments.s307-g42: ز دیوار دژ is a poetic genitive construction meaning 'from the wall of the fortress' (ز = 'from', دیوار = 'wall', دژ = 'fortress'); the ezāfe relation is condensed in poetry. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', سر here means 'top' or 'summit' (the high part of the wall), and بدگمان qualifies that head/top as 'suspicious' or 'wary' — together 'the summit peered like a wary head.'
segments.s307-g43: پدید آمدی is a poetic past phrasing meaning 'appeared' (پدید + آمد(ن)), with the verb placed after the adverbial for emphasis. منجینق is the noun 'trebuchet/engine of siege'. از برش is a prepositional phrase 'from beside it' where از = 'from' and the enclitic ش = 'its/it'; in other words, 'a trebuchet came up from beside it.'
segments.s307-g44: چو here introduces a simile 'as/like'; ژاله = 'dew' provides the image. همی is a frequentative/progressive adverb meaning 'continually/kept (on)'. کوفتی is a past (poetic) form 'struck' or 'smote' (repeated action indicated by همی). بر سرش ('on its head') is the locative object, with the suffix ش = 'its'. Altogether: 'like dew it kept striking on its head.'
segments.s307-g45: پس = 'then/thereupon' (temporal connector). منجنیق is again 'trebuchet'. اندرون رومیان is a locative phrase 'into/within the midst of the Romans' (اندرون = inside; رومیان = the Romans). The sequence shows the movement/location of the siege engine into the enemy area.
segments.s307-g46: This clause describes what was done to the machine: چرخها تنگ بسته میان is a compact, poetic way of saying 'the wheels were tied/fastened tightly together between (them).' تنگ بسته = 'tightly fastened/closed' (تنگ = tight, بسته = fastened/closed). میان = 'between/among'. ابا is tricky here but is best read as marking reluctance/refusal or as an agentive gloss implying that they restrained or secured the wheels; contextually it indicates an action upon the wheels (they made them immobile).
segments.s307-g47: دو صد پیل is the numeral phrase 'two hundred elephants' (numeral before noun). فرمود is the past-tense honorific/verb 'he commanded/ordered'. پس is a temporal connector 'then', and شهریار is 'the king' — the agent; word order is inverted for poetic effect, with the object phrase preceding the verb and subject.
segments.s307-g48: کشیدن here is the infinitive/gerund 'to draw/drag/pull' (the action ordered). ز هر سو = 'from every side' (ز = from, هر سو = every direction/side). به گرد حصار = 'around the fortress' (به گرد = around/about; حصار = the rampart/fortress). Together they specify the king's command: have the elephants drawn around the walls from all sides.
segments.s307-g145: به ... نهادند روی is an idiom: نهادند (past, 3rd pl. of نهادن) + روی (ʼface/directionʼ) with به marking the goal, so literally 'they set (their) face toward'. تاراج و کشتن are verbal-nouns (or nouns used as activities) linked by و; word order places the goal phrase before the verb: 'toward pillage and killing they set (their) face' = 'they began to pillage and kill.'
segments.s307-g146: برآمد is past/inchoative 'arose/issued forth'. خروشیدن‌های is the verbal noun خروشیدن with a pluralizing/collective marker (poetic formation) meaning 'cries/roars', and هوی is an interjection (the shouted exclamation 'hoy'/'hūy') that these cries expressed. The clause means 'cries of “hoy!” arose.'
segments.s307-g147: زَن و کودکان is the coordinated subject 'women and children'. بانگ is a verbal-noun 'a cry/shout' and برداشتند (past, 3rd pl. of برداشتن) here means 'they raised/took up' in the sense 'they uttered/raised a cry' — subject + object + verb: 'women and children raised a cry.'
segments.s307-g148: به ایرانیان جای بگذاشتند uses به to mark the beneficiary/target ('for the Iranians') and جای 'place/space'. بگذاشتند is past (3rd pl.) of گذاشتن with the archaic b- prefix, here meaning 'they left/made (a) place' — 'they left room for the Iranians' or 'they made way for the Iranians.'
segments.s307-g149: چه مایه is an exclamatory phrase ('what a quantity' / 'how many'); زن و کودک is the coordinated subject 'women and children'; نارسید is past negative 'did not arrive/make it' (نرسیدن in past). The clause means 'so many women and children did not reach safety / did not survive.'
segments.s307-g150: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause 'for/that'. زیر 'under' takes پی 'foot/track' (here 'foot') and پیل 'elephant' yields 'under the elephant’s foot/feet'. شد + ناپدید is a verb-like periphrasis: شد 'became' + adjective ناپدید 'vanished' = 'were made vanished / disappeared' (i.e. trampled and gone).
segments.s307-g151: همه شهر توران 'the whole city of Turan' is a noun phrase; گریزان 'fleeing' is a participial/adjectival form, and چو باد is a simile 'like the wind' (چو = 'like', باد = 'wind'). Word order places the simile after the participle for poetic effect.
segments.s307-g152: نیامد کسی را ... یاد is an inversion of the usual construction meaning 'no one remembered' or 'no thought came to anyone'. نیامد (past negative context) is impersonal 'did not come/occur'; کسی را uses را as a topical/definite marker; بر و بوم is a paired phrase 'house and homeland'. Together: 'no one remembered / thought of house and homeland.'
segments.s307-g201: که introduces a subordinate/question clause ('that/when'); چون here functions as an archaic temporal conjunction 'when'. رفت is a past simple (he left) from روفتن, and و links that clause to the following predicate. آرامگاهش is a genitive compound ('his tomb/resting‑place'), کجا is the interrogative 'where', and ست is the copula 'is' placed after the predicate (typical poetic inversion).
segments.s307-g202: نهان گشته is a passive/resultative phrase: گشته (perfect participle/auxiliary) with نهان 'hidden' = 'has become hidden'. ز ایدر is a poetic prepositional phrase ('from here' / 'out of here'), پناهش is a noun with enclitic -ش 'his/its' ('his refuge'), and the clause ends with the interrogative کجا and the copula ست ('where is his refuge?').
segments.s307-g203: ز هر گونه is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in every manner' (ز + هر گونه). گفتند is the past plural 'they said/spoke'. و simply links the clauses, خسرو is the subject of the next verb, and شنید is past 'heard' (he heard). Word order places the reporter phrase before the king's reaction.
segments.s307-g204: نیامد is a negative past/preterite 'did not come' (here: 'did not appear'); همی is a frequentative/continuative adverb 'still/ever' placed before the noun for emphasis. روشنایی is the noun 'brightness/light', and پدید (here postposed) is a verbal/adjectival root meaning 'to appear' — the phrase literally 'light did not come forth'.
segments.s307-g205: به ایرانیان گفت is 'said to the Iranians' — به marks the indirect object/recipient. پیروز شاه is a title phrase 'the victorious king' (adjective + noun) placed after the verb phrase for emphasis (poetic word order). گفت here is past 'said' with implied direct speech or reported content following.
segments.s307-g206: که introduces the content clause ('that'). دشمن is the noun 'enemy'. چو is the archaic subordinating particle 'when' (introducing a temporal clause). آواره گردَد uses آواره 'wandering/displaced' plus گردد (present/jussive of گشتن) meaning 'becomes / is driven to be' — ز گاه literally 'from the time' or 'from that time/occasion' (poetic 'when ...').
segments.s307-g207: ز گیتی is a poetic locative 'in/from the world'. بر او is a prepositional phrase 'upon him' (indicating possession/condition). نام و کام اندکیست is a nominal sentence: 'name and kam (desire, success) are small' — اندکیست (اندک + است) means 'there is little' or 'is slight'. The compound 'نام و کام' = 'fame and fortune/fulfillment'.
segments.s307-g208: ورا ('to him' / 'for him') marks the experiencer. مرگ با زندگانی یکیست is a balanced clause: مرگ 'death' + با 'with' + زندگانی 'life' + یکیست 'is one' — literally 'death with life is one', meaning 'to him death and life are the same.' The adjective/copula یکیست follows the noun phrase in typical poetic order.
segments.s307-g105: This clause poses the rhetorical question ‘what use is…’. که is a clause-introducing particle; با = “with”; باره here means the store/load (provisions) associated with the fortress; دژ = “fortress”; شما را uses شما with the object/topic marker را (addressing ‘your’ fortress); چه کار is the idiom ‘what (is the) use/affair’ (چه = what, کار = use/affair). Word order is poetic and inverted compared with modern Persian but the pieces form ‘what use is your fortress with its stores’.
segments.s307-g106: This is the claim answering the question: سپه را (the army, with را marking it as the theme/object) ز شمشیر = ‘from/against the sword’ (ز = from/against, شمشیر = sword). باید is the existential/necessitative verb 'is necessary/needs' and حصار is a noun used predicatively meaning ‘a bulwark/fortification’. Poetic word order places حصار after باید: ‘the army needs a bulwark against the sword.’
segments.s307-g107: ز بهر = ‘for the sake of’ (ز + بهر, a poetic combination meaning ‘for’/‘on account of’). بر و بوم is a paired expression meaning ‘roof and soil’ → ‘home/land’ (بر used in the fixed phrase), و پیوند خویش = ‘and their bonds/kinship’ where پیوند = bond/link and خویش with the possessive -ش = ‘his/their’. This whole phrase lists what is being defended.
segments.s307-g108: همان here means ‘the same’ or ‘likewise’ linking to the previous items. از پی is an idiom ‘in pursuit of/after’ (از + پی), گنج = ‘treasure’ and فرزند = ‘child/descendant’; خویش (with -ش) is the possessive ‘his/their’. The clause says they pursue/defend also their treasure and children.
segments.s307-g109: ببندیم is a hortative/optative first-person-plural form ‘let us bind/join’ (ب-بند-یم), giving a collective injunction. دامن = ‘hem/skirt’ (here figuratively ‘edge’ or ‘fold’ of garments/territory), یک = ‘one’ (postpositive numeral modifying دامن), اندر = ‘into/among’ and دگر = ‘other’. The phrase literally ‘let us bind one hem into the other’ = ‘let us join together.’
segments.s307-g110: نمانیم is first-person-plural negative-subjunctive/indicative ‘we shall not remain / we will not be left’. بر + دشمنان = ‘on/against the enemies’; بوم = ‘homeland’; و بر repeats the preposition for rhythmic balance (the line breaks or continues after it). The sense is ‘we shall not remain at the mercy of the enemies of the land.’
segments.s307-g111: سپاهی = ‘troops/soldiers’. ز ترکان = ‘from the Turks’ (ز = from/of, ترکان = the Turks). گروها گروه is a repetitive/iterative phrase ‘groups upon groups’ or ‘band after band’ emphasizing many successive bands. The order is emphatic/poetic: ‘troops from the Turks, groups and groups.’
segments.s307-g112: ب + دان here function together as the prepositional demonstrative ‘بدان’ meaning ‘into/unto that’ (a poetic fused form). رخنه = ‘breach/gap’ and رفتند = ‘they went/entered’ (past plural). بر سان کوه = ‘upon/like a mountain’ (بر = on/upon, سان = like/as, کوه = mountain) — the simile describes the manner/force of their advance. Overall: ‘into that breach they went, like a mountain.’
segments.s307-g81: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' introducing causes; here it governs two coordinated causes joined by و. بانگ is used as 'sound/cry' and کمانهای چرخ (literally 'bows of the sky') is a genitival phrase 'the celestial/heavenly bows' (چرخ = sky). دود is the second coordinated cause 'and from the smoke.'
segments.s307-g82: شده (past participle/become) is the predicating verb 'became.' روی خورشید is 'the face/surface of the sun' with روی functioning as 'face/upon'; تابان is a postpositive adjective 'shining' modifying خورشید, and کبود is the resultative adjective 'dusky/blue‑dark' describing that face.
segments.s307-g83: Another coordinated causal phrase begun by ز: عراده و منجنیق ('wagons and catapults') are listed, then a repeated ز introduces گرد. The conjunction و simply links the items; گرد here is the noun 'dust' (not the verb 'to go around').
segments.s307-g84: زمین نیلگون شد: the normal subject‑predicate order 'the earth became blue/indigo' with نیلگون as the predicate adjective and شد as the past copular verb. هوا لاژورد is a paratactic second clause where the copula is omitted (poetic compression): 'the air (became) lapis‑lazuli.'
segments.s307-g85: خروشیدن and بانگ are verbal nouns (or infinitives used as nouns) indicating actions: 'the roaring of the elephant(s)' and 'the cry of the chiefs.' The possessor relation (بانگِ سران) is expressed by noun juxtaposition typical of Persian poetry (the ezāfe is not written). و links the two noises.
segments.s307-g86: درخشیدن is likewise a verbal noun 'the shining/gleam' governing the following objects: 'تیغ و گرز گران' = 'sword and heavy mace.' گران is a postpositive adjective 'heavy/weighty' modifying گرز (and by extension the martial implements in view). The clause presents sights (noisy/visual upheaval).
segments.s307-g87: تو is the 2nd‑person subject 'you.' گفتی functions as the past tense 'you said' introducing reported speech; برآویخت is the verb of the reported clause 'rose up' (perfective). با indicates accompanying manner/instrument and links شید ماه ('love‑mad / love‑struck moon' — a poetic epithet) as the one who rose.
segments.s307-g88: ز introduces the cause 'from/because of' and governs the gerundive nouns: باریدن (the falling/raining) of تیر (arrows). و links گرد سیاه ('black dust') as a coordinated causal phrase; گرد here is the noun 'dust' and سیاه is the attributive adjective 'black.'
segments.s307-g113: به کردار شیران is a preposed simile phrase ('in the manner of lions') where به functions as a manner marker. برآویختند is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person‑plural verb (perfective) with the prefix بر‑ giving the sense 'spring upon/attack'; the subject 'they' is implicit and refers back to the fighters mentioned earlier.
segments.s307-g114: خروش ... برانگیختند places the noun خروش ('a roar/cry') before the verb; از دو رویه is a prepositional phrase ('from/between the two faces/sides') indicating source. برانگیختند is past‑plural and here means 'rose/was aroused' (something rose up), so the clause means 'a roar rose from both sides'.
segments.s307-g115: سواران ترکان is a genitive construction 'the horsemen of the Turks' (noun followed by possessor). به کردار بید is another simile ('in the manner of a willow'); به marks the simile, and بید ('willow') is used metaphorically to suggest bending/trembling.
segments.s307-g116: شده is the past participle/auxiliary 'became' (perfective result) and governs the predicative adjectives that follow. لرزلرزان is a poetic adjective 'trembling/shivering', and دل ناامید is a nominal phrase '(their) hearts hopeless' (elliptical copula: 'were hopeless'). The subject remains 'the horsemen'.
segments.s307-g117: به رستم بفرمود places the indirect object before the verb: به رستم = 'to Rostam', and بفرمود is an honorific past tense verb meaning 'he commanded/said'. پس شهریار is the subject phrase 'then the king' following the verb (poetic inversion), so: 'then the king said to Rostam'.
segments.s307-g118: پیاده هر آن کس که ... is a relative/restrictive phrase: پیاده 'on foot' qualifies the person; هر آن کس که is the correlative 'whoever/anyone who'; بد نامدار is an adjectival phrase 'of bad repute' (بد negating نامدار). The clause is left open and is the object/complement of the king's command.
segments.s307-g119: که پیش اندر آید ... is a relative clause 'whoever comes forward/advances': که = 'who/that', پیش 'forward/in front', اندر آید 'enters/comes in'. بدان رخنه گاه is 'into that breach/place' where the preposition/prefix بـ + demonstrative (بدان) marks motion into 'that breach'; this is an archaic/poetic concatenation ('بـدان').
segments.s307-g120: همیدون بسی نیزه‌ور کینه‌خواه lists what awaits there: همیدون is likely a place‑name or field (here functioning adverbially), بسی is an intensifier 'many' (not matched in the provided sense list), and نیزه‌ور کینه‌خواه are two epithets 'spear‑bearers, vengeance‑seekers' describing the men one will meet. The verb is implied (will be/are) and the phrase modifies the preceding relative clause.
segments.s307-g153: بشد is a past/resultative verb here meaning “became/it came to be”; Persian poetry often places the verb up front. بخت گردان ترکان is a genitive/nominal phrase “the fortune (بخت) of the troop (گردان) of the Turks (ترکان)”; نگون is a postpositive adjective meaning “ill‑fated/dooméd.”
segments.s307-g154: به زاری is an adverbial prepositional phrase “in/with lamentation” (به used adverbially). همه دیدگان is the noun phrase “all the eyes,” and پر ز خون is a predicate structure “full (پر) of blood (ز خون)” — note that پر precedes the of‑phrase in poetry, giving “all eyes full of blood.”
segments.s307-g155: زن و گنج و فرزند is a coordinated list of objects (“women, treasure, and children”). گشته اسیر is a perfect/participial predicate “have become captive / were made captives,” with گشته functioning as the verbal adjective and اسیر the resulting state.
segments.s307-g156: ز گردون is an ablative/causal phrase “by/from the گردون” (here: fate/heaven). روان خسته = “soul/spirit (روان) weary (خسته),” and تن به تیر uses به instrumentally/associatively “with/by arrow(s),” i.e. “the body struck by arrows.” The two noun phrases are linked by و (“and”).
segments.s307-g157: به ایوان برآمد is a simple past action “came up/ascended to the iwan,” with به + place marking destination. پس here temporal “then,” and the proper name افراسیاب is the implied subject (poetic word order places the verb before the subject).
segments.s307-g158: پر از خون modifies دل: “(his) heart full of blood,” where از introduces the material/causal relation (“full of/from blood”) and the following از درد gives the cause “from pain.” Then و links to دیده پرآب, “and (his) eyes full/filled with water (tears).” Note poetic adjacency: predicates like پر often appear before their following از‑phrase.
segments.s307-g159: بر آن باره بر شد is an idiomatic poetic phrase “on that occasion it happened / it came to pass.” که introduces the content clause. بد کاخ اوی = “his bad/ill‑fated palace,” where بد precedes کاخ as an attributive adjective and اوی is the possessive/third‑person form “his.”
segments.s307-g160: بیامد سوی شارستان means “he came toward the city/town” (بیامد = came, سوی marks direction). کرد روی is a small verbal phrase “made/turned (his) face” — کرد as “make/do” plus روی “face” = “turned his face (toward it),” a common idiom for turning toward a place.
segments.s307-g329: کز ایشان شه نامبردار کین groups a relative/prepositional phrase that identifies the subject: کز (archaic preposition) + ایشان 'them' = 'from among them / of them'; شه نامبردار is the noun phrase 'the famed king' (شه = king, نامبردار = renowned). کین (a noun) sits at the end of the segment and will be taken up as the object/theme 'hatred/vengeance' in the clause that follows.
segments.s307-g330: نخواهد is the negative future/irrealis auxiliary here meaning 'will not' (classical poetic usage). ز بهر functions as a two‑word prepositional idea ('for/toward' or 'on account of') and takes جهان‌آفرین 'the world‑creator' as its object; the whole segment says that the hatred (کین) will not be directed toward/for the world‑creator.
segments.s307-g331: چنین گفت is a fixed formula 'thus said / said thus.' کیخسرو هوشمند is the speaker in apposition: کیخسرو (the legendary king) followed by the epithet هوشمند 'the wise.' Word order is Subject (name + epithet) after the reported-verb phrase, which is common in poetic narrative.
segments.s307-g332: که introduces reported speech or a clause 'that / which.' هر چیز = 'whatever thing / anything.' کان نیست is a compact classical expression functioning as 'is not' (کان here is archaic; combined with نیست yields 'is not / does not exist'); ما را پسند = 'pleasing to us' with را marking پسند as an object/topic. The whole segment means 'that anything which is not pleasing to us...' (trailing on to the next sentence).
segments.s307-g333: نیاریم here is a first‑person plural negated verb meaning 'we will not do / we do not...' (poetic form). کس را = 'anyone' with را as the object marker. همان بد به روی is an adverbial phrase 'just/merely for a bad face' (همان = 'simply/that very', بد = 'bad', به روی = 'because of the face / on account of appearance'). The segment says 'we will not ... anyone merely because of a bad appearance.'
segments.s307-g334: وگر چند باشد is concessive 'and even if / though although he be somewhat...' (وگر = 'if / but if', چند = 'some / however much', باشد = subjunctive/present 'is / may be'). جگر کینه‌جوی is a noun phrase 'a heart (جگر) seeking vengeance' — i.e. 'vengeful at heart.' The segment concedes that even if someone is inwardly vengeful, the speaker still will not condemn them for mere appearance.
segments.s307-g335: چو = 'when' (introducing a temporal clause). از کار آن نامدار بلند = 'from/considering the deeds/works of that famed lofty one' — از + کار = 'about/regarding the deeds', آن نامدار بلند is the descriptive noun phrase 'that renowned, high/lofty one.' The phrase sets the topic/time of the following deliberation.
segments.s307-g336: براندیشم is a first‑person singular present/subjunctive 'I consider / I deliberate.' اینم = 'this (thing)' with enclitic -م marking 'to me' or 'this of mine' in poetic usage. نیاید پسند here is an impersonal/poetic negative 'does not come to be liked' = 'is not pleasing.' Together: 'When I consider those deeds, this does not please me.'
segments.s307-g273: همان پروریده بتان طراز is a noun phrase in which همان ('the same/that') modifies پروریده ('nurtured/cultivated'), which in turn qualifies بتان (here 'beauties, beloveds' used like 'idol‑like women'). طراز functions as an adjectival/original‑noun modifier meaning 'in the pattern/style (of idols)' — together: 'those well‑reared, idol‑fashioned beauties.' Word order (determiner — participle — noun — postmodifier) is normal for Persian poetic diction.
segments.s307-g274: بر این‌گونه بردند پیشش نماز is a verbal clause where بردند (they carried/brought) is the main verb; بر این‌گونه ('in/according to this manner') is an adverbial phrase modifying بردند, پیشش ('before him/in his presence') is a locative complement, and نماز ('prayer') is the object/what was performed or presented. The word order places the manner first for emphasis (poetic inversion).
segments.s307-g275: همه یکسره زار بگریستند is a full clause: همه ('everyone') is the subject, یکسره ('completely/utterly') and زار ('miserably/with lamentation') are adverbial/modifying elements describing the manner and intensity of بگریستند ('they wept'). The auxiliary prefix ب‑ on the verb is fused into the past form بگریستند (classical/perfective past).
segments.s307-g276: بدان شوربختی همی زیستند is another clause: بدان here functions adverbially ('in/with that'), where the particle ب‑ plus دان form a classical locative/relational construction meaning 'in that.' شوربختی is the noun 'misfortune', همی is a habitual/continuous marker meaning 'still/continually,' and زیستند is the past of 'to live' ('they lived/continued to live'). Together they mean 'they continued to live in that misfortune.'
segments.s307-g277: کسی کو ندیدست جز کام و ناز is an elliptic, classical line: کسی کو here functions as an indefinite/negative 'no one' (classical usage), ندیدست is a perfective/passive‑feeling form meaning 'was seen / is seen' (negated by the construction), and جز introduces the exception 'except (for) کام و ناز' ('desire and coquettishness'). The phrase thus states that nothing was seen except desire and coquetry. Note the archaic relative/indefinite use of کو.
segments.s307-g278: بر او بر ببخشای روز نیاز is a plea/clause: بر او ('upon him/for him') is the indirect object, the repeated بر adds emphasis or direction, and ببخشای is an optative/imprecative form meaning 'grant' (imperative/entreaty). روز نیاز literally 'the day of supplication/need' is the thing being asked for. Word order (object — verb — complement) follows poetic inversion; the sense is 'grant him the day of supplication.'
segments.s307-g279: همی is a classical iterative particle that gives a continuous/progressive sense ('kept doing'). خواندند is 3rd‑person plural past ('they sang/performed'); the direct object is آفرینی, here the exclamatory word “Afarīn” (an interjection of praise). به درد is a prepositional phrase ('at/with sorrow' or 'because of grief') modifying the verb phrase.
segments.s307-g280: که‌ای is an archaic/exclamatory vocative construction (roughly 'O/ah, you'); it introduces an address. نیک‌دل is an epithet preceding the name (adjective + noun order), خسرو is the proper name, and رادمرد is a second epithet ('generous/man of integrity'). The sequence is vocative + adjective + name + epithet, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s307-g185: This is a noun phrase with a relative clause: 'در ایوان' = 'in the iwan', followed by 'که ... بود' which modifies ایوان. برآورده بود is a past participle + auxiliary (literal: 'had been brought/erected'); the second در marks location 'in the fortress'. Word order places the relative clause after the noun it describes, as usual in Persian poetry.
segments.s307-g186: یکی here functions as an indefinite subject meaning 'someone/a certain one' before the noun راه ('a passage/path'). 'راه زیر زمین' is a compound expression 'a way under the earth' and کرده بود is the perfect participle + auxiliary 'had made' (کرده = made/done, بود = auxiliary past). The clause is past-tense and the agent is indefinite.
segments.s307-g187: 'از آن نامداران' is a partitive phrase 'from those renowned ones'; 'دو صد' (two hundred) is the numeral object, and برگزید is the past tense verb 'chose/selected'. Persian often places such object phrases before the verb; the explicit subject is omitted (understood from context).
segments.s307-g188: 'بر آن راه' = 'on that road/path'. بی‌راه شد is an idiomatic structure: بی‌راه ('off the road/astray') followed by شد ('became/went'), and ناپدید is the resultant state 'vanished'. The sequence places the state-word (بی‌راه) before شد, then the resulting adjective (ناپدید).
segments.s307-g189: وزآنجای is a fused prepositional phrase 'from that place/spot' (وز = از + وز poetic). 'راه بیابان گرفت' literally 'the road took (on) desert' — گرفت here conveys 'became/seized by' so the meaning is 'the way became a wilderness'. The verb گرفت is simple past and marks a change of state.
segments.s307-g190: 'همه کشورش' = 'his whole country/realm' (همه before the noun emphasizes entirety; ش is the possessive clitic 'his'). ماند is past 'remained/was left', and 'اندر شگفت' is a prepositional phrase 'in wonder' or 'in astonishment'. Word order is topic ('whole realm') + predicate ('was left in wonder').
segments.s307-g191: نشانی is the object 'a sign/trace'. ندادش is the negative past verb 'did not give' with the clitic -ش 'to him' attached, so together 'did not give him'. کس here functions as 'no one' (negative indefinite) and اندر جهان 'in the world' localizes the statement; the word order is object + verb(with clitic) + subject + locative, a poetic arrangement but grammatical.
segments.s307-g192: بدان گونه = 'in that manner/way' (بدان is an archaic/demonstrative compound meaning 'to/with that' + گونه 'manner'). آواره شد is 'became a wanderer/was made wandering' (آواره = wandering/displaced; شد = became). در نهان = 'in secret/hidden'. The clause thus states manner + resulting state + location/adverb.
segments.s307-g73: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. برداشت is a past tense (3rd sg.) of برداشتن meaning 'he raised/took up'; the phrase از پیش یزدان literally 'from before God' (= 'in God's presence') follows the verb. سرش is 'his head' where the possessive clitic -ش attaches to سر and appears after the verb phrase in this poetic word order.
segments.s307-g74: بپوشید is the past (3rd sg.) literary form of پوشیدن 'he put on/wore'. به introduces the thing put on, جوشن 'armor/chainmail'. روشن 'bright/gleaming' modifies جوشن but is post‑verbal here for poetic effect, and برش = بر + ش ('on it/him') is a clitic referring back (i.e. 'on it/him'); because of lemma mismatch in the list, برش's listed senses don't cover this pronominal use.
segments.s307-g75: کمر بر میان بست is literally 'he fastened/bound the belt at his middle' (کمر = waist/belt; بر میان = 'on the middle'). بست is past 'he bound/tied'. و برجست زود follows with و 'and' plus برجست (past) 'he rose/appeared', with زود 'soon/quickly' as an adverb modifying the verb.
segments.s307-g76: به جنگ اندر آمد is 'he came/entered into battle' (اندر آمد = entered; به جنگ marks the destination/scene). به کردار دود is a simile: به 'in', کردار 'manner, conduct' (here 'in the manner of'), دود 'smoke' — 'in the manner/shape of smoke' (i.e. swift or swirling like smoke).
segments.s307-g77: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense 'he commanded/ordered'. تا here introduces the subordinate (purpose/result) clause 'so that/that'. سخت is an adverb 'strongly/severely'. بر هر دری is 'against every door/gate' (بر = on/against, هر = every). Note: the lemma senses supplied for دری do not include the 'door/gate' meaning present in the text, so that sense is not available in the provided list.
segments.s307-g78: به جنگ اندر آید is the content of the commanded clause: به جنگ 'to/into battle', اندر آید (آید here is a present/subjunctive form used in subordinate clauses after تا) 'should come/enter'. یکی لشکری means 'one troop' or 'a certain body of troops' (یکی = one/a certain, لشکری = troops/troop).
segments.s307-g79: بدان is the deictic 'to/at that(those)' (here tokenized as ب + دان in the word list; the provided lemmas don't align perfectly). چوب و نفط 'wood and oil' are the things acting as fuel, آتش اندر زدند literally 'they put fire into' or 'they set on fire'—زدند is past plural 'they struck/they set'. Overall: 'they set fire to that wood and lamp-oil.'
segments.s307-g80: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. برشان = بر + شان 'upon/over them' (شان = their/them). همی is a continuous/emphatic particle 'kept/was repeatedly'. سنگ‌بر سر زدند is 'they struck/threw stones at/on the head(s)' — سنگ‌بر here functions as 'stone' + prepositional relation; the provided lemma for سنگ‌بر lists 'stone‑breaker' and thus does not include the simple 'stone' sense, so that item is marked as unavailable in the sense list.
segments.s307-g97: به here is the preposition 'for / toward' introducing the purpose or aim (a shout 'for victory'). پیروزی is a noun 'victory' and is the object of به. از + لشکر شهریار is a genitive phrase 'from the king's army' (از = 'from', لشكر = 'army', شهریار = 'king'), giving the source of the shout.
segments.s307-g98: برآمد is a past-tense verb 'arose/issued forth' (3rd person). خروشیدن is a verbal noun (masdar) used as the thing that 'arose' — 'a roar/cry' — and کارزار ('battle') follows as its qualifier ('the roar of battle' or 'battle-cry'), linked by juxtaposition typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s307-g99: سوی is a preposition 'toward(s)' and introduces the direction. رخنه دژ is a genitive phrase 'the breach of the fortress' (رخنه = 'breach', دژ = 'fortress'). نهادند is the past plural verb 'they set/placed' and with روی (face) the idiom 'روی نهادند' means 'they turned their faces toward' (they directed themselves).
segments.s307-g100: بیامد is a past verb 'came'. دمان (here) gives a locative sense 'to the hem/edge / skirt' (a place or edge of the field) indicating where he arrived. رستم is the subject/name, immediately followed by the epithet کینه‌جوی, an adjective in apposition meaning 'vengeful / revenge-seeking' that characterises Rostam.
segments.s307-g101: خبر is 'news' or 'report'. شد here functions as an intransitive past 'happened/came to pass' (so 'news came'). به نزدیک افراسیاب is a prepositional phrase 'to/near Afrasiab' (به = 'to', نزدیک = 'near', افراسیاب = proper name), indicating who received the news.
segments.s307-g102: کجا here introduces the content of the news and functions like an archaic subordinating particle ('that/where/that which'); باره is an adverbial/nominal particle 'on the occasion' or 'in that instance' introducing the event. شارستان is 'city/town' and شد خراب is a compound where شد = 'became' and خراب = 'ruined/destroyed' — 'the town became ruined' (i.e., was laid waste).
segments.s307-g103: پس = 'then' (temporal). افراسیاب is the subject. اندر آمد is an archaic compound 'came in / entered' (اندر = 'in/within', آمد = 'came'). چو = 'like/as' introduces a simile, and گرد here (literary) denotes a dust-cloud/whirl, so چو گرد = 'like a whirlwind' or 'as a cloud of dust'.
segments.s307-g104: به ... و به ... are repeated prepositional phrases 'to Jahan and to Garsivaz' (each به = 'to' followed by a proper name). آواز کرد is a light-verb construction: آواز = 'voice/call' and کرد = past 'did/made', together 'he called out / raised his voice' (i.e., he shouted toward them).
segments.s307-g313: This is a series of verbal-noun phrases joined by و. هر «به» introduces a noun/nominalized action (به خواری … به خون ریختن). خون ریختن is a compound of the noun خون + the verbal-noun ریختن, so it means “the spilling of blood.” The whole segment lists wrongs in parallel: humiliation, wounds, and blood‑shedding.
segments.s307-g314: چه is an exclamatory/ interrogative particle here (“what/how”), and آویختن is a verbal noun (“to hang”). بر introduces the target/recipient (بر بی‌گنه = upon the innocent). خیره modifies آویختن, describing the manner as ‘staring/gaping.’ The phrase therefore expresses (rhetorically) hanging/attaching oneself in a gaping way onto the innocent.
segments.s307-g315: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause (“for/that”), and از + plural شهریاران here means “of/for the kings” (i.e. with respect to rulers). سزاوار نیست is a fixed predicate meaning “is not appropriate/fitting.” In short: “that/of kings it is not fitting.”
segments.s307-g316: بریدن is the verbal noun ‘to cut/ to behead’ (here causative/agentive action). سری = “head” (object of بریدن). کان is an archaic connective/relative here linking to the following predicate (roughly ‘which/that’). گنهکار نیست is the negated predicate “is not a sinner.” Together: “to behead a head which is not sinful is not fitting.”
segments.s307-g317: ترا (ت + را) here addresses the addressee (“to you”) combined with the vocative شهریارا (“O king”). جز اینست جای literally ‘except this is place’ → ‘there is no place except this.’ The clause is a vocative plus a statement ‘apart from this…’.
segments.s307-g318: نماند is the negated perfect/present sense ‘is left/ remains not’ and is placed before کسـی (typical classical order: verb before (indefinite) subject). کسی with this negation yields ‘no one’ (i.e. nobody remains). در + سپنجی سرای is a locative prepositional phrase: ‘in the spongy house’ (سپنجی modifies سرای).
segments.s307-g319: هم آن کن is an emphatic imperative: ‘do that very thing.’ که introduces a relative clause ‘which/that’ and پرسد (here present/future) means ‘asks’ (the Creator will ask). ز تو marks the object/source of the asking (‘of you’). کردگار is the agent ‘the Creator/God.’
segments.s307-g320: نپیچی is a negative imperative (ن + پیچی) meaning ‘do not turn/swerve.’ از آن شرم روز شمار is the object/phrase from which one must not turn away: از آن = ‘from that,’ شرم روز شمار = ‘the shame/modesty of the day of reckoning’ (روز شمار = day of counting/reckoning). Together: ‘do not turn away from that shame on the day of judgment.’
segments.s307-g129: پیاده (preverbal adverb) = 'on foot'; بیامد is past tense, 3rd person singular 'he came'. به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner/with the conduct (of...)' and گرد here functions adverbially 'around / in a circling way' — the phrase as a whole describes how he approached (poetic inversion places manner after the verb).
segments.s307-g130: درفش سیه را is the definite direct object (درفش 'standard' modified by the adjective سیه 'black', followed by the object marker را). نگون‌سار is an adjectival/participial form meaning 'downcast/overturned' and کرد is the past-tense verb 'made/caused' (causative/lexical 'do' used to effect the change). Together 'نگون‌سار کرد' = 'caused to be cast down/overturned'.
segments.s307-g131: نشان is the head noun 'insignia/mark'; سپهدار ایران is a genitive unit 'the commander of Iran' modifying نشان (noun + possessor). بنفش is a postpositive adjective 'purple' describing نشان — Persian often places adjectives after the noun in poetry.
segments.s307-g132: بر آن باره زد = 'on that occasion/instance (بر آن باره) he struck/struck at (زد)'; شیر پیکر درفش is the object phrase 'the banner bearing the figure of a lion' (شیر پیکر = 'lion‑formed/ lion‑figured' modifying درفش). Word order places the temporal/occasional phrase before زد and the object after the verb.
segments.s307-g133: به پیروزی is a prepositional phrase 'at/on the victory' or 'for the victory'; شاه ایران is a genitive 'the King of Iran' modifying پیروزی; سپاه is the subject 'the army' (noun phrase order is topical — result/occasion + possessor + subject).
segments.s307-g134: برآمد is past 'arose/issued forth'; خروشیدن here is a verbal noun/infinitive 'roaring/crying' functioning as the thing that arose ('a roar arose'). از رزمگاه is the source phrase 'from the battlefield'. The construction is a common Persian pattern where برآمد + verbal noun = 'a ... arose/was heard'.
segments.s307-g135: فراوان is an adverb 'in large numbers/abundantly'; ز (poetic form of از) marks origin 'from/of'; توران سپه = 'Turan's host' (inverted genitive order typical in poetry); کشته شد is a passive past 'were killed'. The whole line means that many from Turan's army were slain.
segments.s307-g136: سر بخت is a compound meaning 'the head/top of fortune' i.e. 'good fortune' or 'prime luck'; تورانیان (postposed) marks whose fortune it is ('of the Turanians'); گشته شد is perfect‑like passive 'has become / was turned' (گشته = participle, شد = auxiliary). Together: 'the Turanians' luck was turned/ruined.'
segments.s307-g377: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'after that' (از + آن + پس). به لشکر is the indirect object 'to the army' (به + لشکر). بفرمود شاه is an honorific past verb phrase: بفرمود = 'he commanded/said' and شاه (the subject) follows in poetic word order.
segments.s307-g378: گشادن is an infinitival/commandive verbal noun 'to open' used as the content of the king's order. در + گنج توران is a locative/genitive phrase 'in the treasure of Turan' (در marking location, گنج = treasure, توران = Turan). سپاه at the end restates or emphasizes the addressee 'the army' — a poetic repetition rather than a new argument.
segments.s307-g379: جز از is an exclusion phrase 'except from/for'. گنج ویژه is a noun + adjective 'the special/privileged treasure'. رد افراسیاب is a genitive/attributive phrase attached to گنج, an archaic poetic compound indicating a particular reserve belonging to Afrasiab (رد here is an older noun in apposition).
segments.s307-g380: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. کس را نبود is an impersonal negative existential 'there was no one' (کس = anyone/no one, را = object/emphasis marker, نبود = 'was not'). اندر آن دست یاب is a locative + compound verb meaning 'to gain/lay hands on it' (اندر آن = 'in/there', دست یاب = 'attain/obtain' or 'get by hand'), completing the relative clause.
segments.s307-g381: ببخشید is a past tense verb 'he bestowed/gave away' (third person). دیگر همه is the object 'the rest/all the others' (دیگر = the remaining/other, همه = all). بر سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'upon/to the army' indicating recipients (بر used here like 'to/among').
segments.s307-g382: The correlative construction چه ... و چه ... enumerates items 'whether... or...' and lists the spoils: گنج (treasure), سلیح (weapons/arms), تخت (throne), کلاه (hat/helmet). و simply connects the items; this is a loose appositive list describing 'دیگر همه'.
segments.s307-g383: ز هر سو is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from every side' (ز = from, هر سو = every direction/side). پراگنده is an adjective 'scattered/dispersed'. بی + مر is an archaic/poetic phrase 'without X' (بی = without; مر here is an older element whose exact nuance is poetic/elliptical in context), followed by سپاه 'army' as the noun being described.
segments.s307-g384: ز ترکان is an agent/source phrase 'from the Turks'. بیامد is the simple past 'came' (3rd person). به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to near the king' meaning 'approached the king' (به + نزدیک + شاه).
segments.s307-g49: یکی is an indefinite subject here meaning 'one / someone'. کنده‌ای is a noun/participial form from کندن (to dig) meaning 'a digger' or 'one who digs' and is linked to the following verb; زیر باره درون is an adverbial phrase (زیر = 'under', باره = 'burden/load', درون = 'inside/within') and specifies where the digging took place.
segments.s307-g50: بکند is the narrative/past verbal form (classical prefix بـ + کرد/کند) meaning 'he/one dug' (here part of the reported action). و is the coordinating 'and'. نهادند is past tense, third-person plural 'they placed / they set'. زیرش is زیر plus the third-person enclitic -ش = 'under it/under him'. ستون is the direct object 'pillar/column' placed under the burden.
segments.s307-g51: بد at the start is a predicative/exclamatory adjective meaning 'bad/ill/woe' (used to condemn). آن منکری is a demonstrative + agent noun 'that denier / that shameful man'. باره مانده is a participial phrase: باره = 'the burden' and مانده (past participle / adjective) = 'having remained / stranded', and به پای ('به' + 'پای') places that state 'at the foot / at the feet' (i.e. at the base).
segments.s307-g52: بدان here functions as a fused prepositional-demonstrative 'with/those' (classical ب + آن = 'with that / with those'), introducing the instrument. نیزه‌ها is the plural 'spears'. برگرفته is a participle meaning 'taken / seized up' (from گرفتن). ز جای is a poetic genitive/ablative 'from their place(s)'. Together: 'with those spears taken from their places.'
segments.s307-g53: پس is a temporal/consequential adverb 'then/thereupon'. آلود (from آلودن/آلاییدن) is used as a past verb 'they smeared / they stained'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon', چوب 'wood', and نفط سیاه 'black oil' (نفط = oil/petroleum, سیاه = black), so the clause means 'they smeared black oil upon the wood.'
segments.s307-g54: بدین گونه is an idiom meaning 'in this manner / thus' (بدین = 'with this', گونه = 'manner'). فرمود is past third-person 'he said / commanded'. بیدار شاه is the subject/title 'Bīdar (the) king' or 'the awake king' (here a proper epithet/title), so the phrase reads 'thus said King Bīdar.'
segments.s307-g55: به یک‌سو is an adverbial phrase 'to one side / aside'. بر از in this classical/poetic sequence marks origin/agent (something like 'from' or 'by means of'), so بر از منجنیق و ز تیر reads 'by/from the catapult and from the arrow(s)'. منجنیق is 'catapult / siege-engine', و = 'and', ز is the poetic preposition 'from', تیر = 'arrow'.
segments.s307-g56: رخ 'face' is the head noun. سرکشان is a descriptive plural meaning 'the proud / rebellious' (here qualifying رخ); گشته is the perfect/participial 'have become / were turned'. همچون = 'like/as', and زریر is a proper name used as a simile. The clause says the faces of the proud were turned, becoming like Zarrir's.
segments.s307-g217: A nominal phrase placed before the verb: هم‌آواز functions as a premodifier meaning “accompanied/along with” (literally “in unison”), followed by پوشیده‌رویان, a plural noun ‘veiled‑faced/modest ones’. The possessive marker اوی after the noun is an older/poetic form meaning ‘his/her’ (postposed possessive), so the whole phrase reads ‘accompanied by his veiled‑faced ones.’
segments.s307-g218: نخواهم is the present (first‑person) negative of خواستن ‘to want/wish’ meaning ‘I do not want’. It is followed by the complementizer که and the present subjunctive/jussive آید ‘that he come’ (standard Persian pattern نخواستن + که + subjunctive). ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’, ایوان ‘portico/veranda’, and به + کوی marks motion ‘into/to the lane’. The clause therefore states the speaker’s wish against that coming.
segments.s307-g219: Simple clause where نگهبان (guard) is the noun tied to the verb فرستاد ‘sent’ (Persian permits object/agent before or after the verb); سوی گله is a directional prepositional phrase ‘toward the flock/herd’ (سوی = toward, گله = herd/crowd). The verb فرستاد is past tense ‘he/they sent’.
segments.s307-g220: که is a relative/complementizer ‘that/who’, introducing the subordinate clause. بودند is the past plural of بودن ‘they were’. گرد here means ‘around/about’, دژ is ‘fortress’, اندر is the older form of در ‘in/within’, and یله is an adjective/adverb meaning ‘loose/unrestrained’ or ‘in disorder’. Together: ‘that were about the fortress, loose/roaming.’
segments.s307-g221: ز خویشان او is a genitive phrase ‘of his relatives’ (ز = poetic ‘from/of’, خویشان = relatives, او = he/his). کس is used with the negative verb to mean ‘no one’. نیازرد is an archaic negative past form (نـ + آزارَ/آزارَد) meaning ‘did not harm/afflict’; syntactically کس is the subject and شاه the object: ‘No one of his relatives harmed the king.’
segments.s307-g222: چنانچون بود = ‘just as it/ he was’ (چنانچون = ‘just as/so’ + بود past ‘was’). در خور پیشگاه is an idiomatic locution: در + خور + پیشگاه conveys ‘in that which befits the presence/court’ (پیشگاه = royal presence/court). The whole fragment qualifies the preceding clause: ‘just as he was in a manner fitting the court.’
segments.s307-g223: چو introduces a temporal clause ‘when’. زان گونه = ‘that kind/that manner’ (زان = ‘that’, گونه = ‘manner/kind’). دیدند is past ‘they saw’. کردار اوی is ‘his conduct/behavior’ with the archaic postposed possessive اوی ‘his’. Word order is typical of Persian temporal/subordinate clauses: temporal marker + demonstrative phrase + past verb + noun phrase commenting on what was seen.
segments.s307-g224: سپه شد is a simple nominal predicate ‘the army became’. سراسر پر از is a compound predicate meaning ‘completely/full of’ (سراسر = throughout/entire, پر = full). از introduces the content (of), and گفت و گوی is a paired noun-phrase ‘talk and sayings’ — an idiomatic expression for ‘conversation/discussion’. The verb شد is the copula ‘became’ linking subject to the predicate nominal.
segments.s307-g169: This short clause lists two contrasted states: 'یکی شاد' (one [person] happy) followed by 'دیگر پر از درد و رنج' (the other full of pain and suffering). Persian allows the copula to be omitted: the predicate adjectives follow the pronoun-like 'یکی' and 'دیگر'. 'پر از' is a fixed adjectival phrase meaning 'full of'.
segments.s307-g170: This clause is a simile/comparison introduced by 'چنانچون' ('just as'); 'بود' is the past copula linking the comparison to 'رسم و رای سپنج' (the custom and counsel of سپنج). Word order is Subject/Circumstance (چنانچون) + verb (بود) + noun phrase (رسم و رای سپنج).
segments.s307-g171: The clause begins with the subordinating conjunction 'چو' meaning 'when', followed by the proper name 'افراسیاب' and the adverb 'آنچنان' ('in that way'); 'دید' is the simple past 'saw' and 'کار' (= 'the matter/affair') names what he observed. Order is temporal conjunction + subject + comment + past verb + object.
segments.s307-g172: This is a nominal phrase describing the consequence: 'چنان هول' ('such terror') joined by 'و' to 'برگشتن کارزار' — here 'برگشتن' acts as a verbal-noun/infinitive meaning 'turning back/retreat' and 'کارزار' is 'battle'. The sequence expresses result: such terror and the battle's turning/retreat.
segments.s307-g173: This is a catalogue introduced by the negative 'نه' repeated for emphasis: 'نه پور و برادر' (neither son nor brother) then 'نه بوم و نه بر' (neither homeland nor بر). Each pair uses 'و' to link two nouns; the repeated 'نه' negates each pair. The word 'بر' here is a lexical item whose sense is unclear from the provided sense list (so treated separately).
segments.s307-g174: Continuation of the negative catalogue: 'نه تاج و نه گنج و نه تخت و کمر' lists crown, treasure, throne, and girdle/belt as also absent. 'کمر' here functions as 'girdle/belt' (an item of regalia) rather than literal 'waist' because it appears in a list of royal insignia.
segments.s307-g175: 'همی' is an adverb meaning 'continually/kept on' and modifies the verb 'گفت' (here 'he said/kept saying'); the prepositional phrase 'با دل پر از داغ و درد' ('with a heart full of burning grief and pain') describes his emotional state. Note that 'گفت' is a past-tense verb here (but the provided senses did not list the verbal sense).
segments.s307-g176: This is an indirect question introduced by 'که' ('that/which') followed by the idiomatic compound 'چرخ فلک' ('wheel of heaven' = fate). 'خیره' here qualifies 'چرخ فلک' (an adjectival use like 'astonishing' or 'dazed'), 'با من' is the accusative/agent phrase 'to/with me', and 'چه کرد' is the past interrogative 'what did (it) do'.
segments.s307-g161: دو بهره ز جنگاوران کشته دید is a compact past‑tense clause: دو بهره ('two portions' / 'two [men]') followed by ز + noun marks source or partitive ('of the warriors'), کشته is a past passive participle describing those two, and دید is the 3rd‑person singular past tense verb 'he saw'. Word order is essentially [object phrase] + [verb], with the subject omitted (understood from context).
segments.s307-g162: دگر یکسر از جنگ برگشته دید: دگر 'the others/another', یکسر 'entirely' (an adverbial intensifier), از جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'from the battle/war', برگشته is a past participle 'returned', and دید is the reporting verb in past tense. The clause reads as '[he] saw others entirely returned from the battle'; again the subject is omitted and the participle + prepositional phrase precede the verb.
segments.s307-g163: خروش سواران و بانگ زنان is two coordinated noun+genitive phrases: خروش سواران 'the roar/clamour of the riders' and بانگ زنان 'the cry/shout of the women'. The noun (خروش / بانگ) comes before the genitive noun (سواران / زنان), a normal Persian order for 'X of Y'. The conjunction و links the two parallel phrases.
segments.s307-g164: هم از پشت پیلان تبیره زنان is an adverbial/prepositional phrase modifying the preceding sound: هم 'also', از پشت پیلان 'from behind the elephants' (پشت = 'rear/behind'), then تبیره زنان is a compound noun phrase naming the people making the sound (literally 'tabireh‑women' in the text). The exact meaning of تبیره here is archaic/uncertain; syntactically زنان is a plural agent (here 'those striking' or 'women who strike/beat'), so the whole phrase means 'also from behind the elephants, the (instrument‑)beaters'.
segments.s307-g165: همی پیل بر زندگان راندند: همی is a narrative/adverbial particle (here marking ongoing or emphatic action), پیل is the subject 'the elephants', بر زندگان is a prepositional phrase 'against/on the living', راندند is the 3rd‑person plural past tense 'they drove' or 'they charged'. The clause is S (subject) + oblique phrase + V in past tense.
segments.s307-g166: همی پشتشان بر زمین ماندند: again همی is narrative/emphatic; پشتشان is پشت + possessive clitic شان 'their backs' (pinned directly to the noun), بر زمین is locative 'on the ground', and ماندند is 3rd‑person plural past 'they remained / were left'. The verb is intransitive and the clause says that their backs lay upon the ground (i.e. they were prostrate or slain).
segments.s307-g167: همه شارستان دود و فریاد دید: همه شارستان 'the whole town' (subject), دود و فریاد 'smoke and outcry' (coordinated objects), and دید is the past‑tense reporting verb. Word order is subject + object(s) + verb, with the coordination showing multiple things observed by the city's people.
segments.s307-g168: همان کشتن و غارت و باد دید: همان 'the same' or 'that very', followed by a coordinated list of nouns/gerunds (کشتن 'killing', غارت 'plundering', and باد — an archaic/ambiguous item here) and the verb دید 'they saw'. Grammatically this repeats and specifies the disasters witnessed; the final noun (باد) is obscure in meaning/context and functions as part of the list.
segments.s307-g1: یکی introduces one item in an enumerated list ('one (item)'). تاج is the noun 'crown'. دادش is the past verb داد with the enclitic pronoun -ش = 'him', so literally 'gave him'; the subject is omitted and is understood from context. زبرجد نگار is a postpositive descriptive phrase: زبرجد names the gem (peridot) and نگار is a poetic adjectival/ornamental tag meaning 'adorned with / decorated by' that modifies تاج.
segments.s307-g2: یکی again enumerates a single item. طوق (necklace/collar) is the noun and زرین is the following adjective 'golden'. و links this phrase to the next; دو is the numeral 'two' modifying گوشوار 'earrings'. Word order is numeral + noun + adjective (common in Persian poetry) and then the coordinating structure.
segments.s307-g3: همانگه is an adverb meaning 'at that moment' or 'immediately'. بشد is the classical past/inchoative 'it happened / he came' (here functioning as a past action). جهن is a proper name (the subject), and پیش پدر is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before his father' that completes the clause. Note the verb appears before the proper name (verb–subject order) as often in older Persian.
segments.s307-g4: بگفت is the past tense 'he said / he told'. آن سخنها = 'those words' (آن + plural سخنها), and همه 'all' emphasizes totality. در به در is an idiomatic doubled phrase: each در is the 'door' sense, so در به در literally 'door to door' and idiomatically implies wandering, distress, or being helpless; it functions adverbially modifying how/with what state he spoke.
segments.s307-g5: ز here means 'because of / from (on account of)' and takes the following noun as its object. پاسخ is 'reply/answer'. برآشفت is a past tense verb meaning 'became angry / flew into a rage'. افراسیاب is the subject (proper name) placed after the verb, a common order in classical Persian.
segments.s307-g6: سواری 'rider, horseman' is the noun/topic. ز ترکان 'from the Turks' is a prepositional phrase modifying the rider. کجا is the interrogative 'where / how' placed before the verb; یافت is the past of یافتن 'to find' (here rhetorical: 'could find' or 'would find'), and خواب is the object 'sleep'. The clause asks rhetorically how a Turkish rider could find any sleep.
segments.s307-g7: ببخشید is the past of بخشیدن in the sense 'he gave / bestowed' (not 'forgave' here). گنج درم are the direct objects: گنج 'treasure' and درم 'dirhams (money)'. بر سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'upon the army' indicating the beneficiaries/recipients. Word order is verb + objects + prepositional phrase.
segments.s307-g8: همان is a demonstrative/adjectival 'the same / that' modifying the following list. The nouns ترگ (rag), شمشیر (sword), تخت (throne), and کلاه (hat/helmet) are coordinated with و 'and' between items — a simple enumerative noun phrase describing additional equipment or items given/mentioned.
segments.s307-g121: This is an instrumental list introduced by the initial particle (a poetic/older form here written 'ابا' corresponding to 'با' = 'with'), followed by coordinated nouns joined with 'و'. The nouns enumerate weapons/kit (quiver, sword, arrow, axe); the list functions as an adjunct describing what accompanies the rider in the next segment.
segments.s307-g122: The noun 'سوار' (horseman) is qualified by the participial/adjectival 'ایستاده' (standing), describing his posture. 'پس' is an adverb meaning 'afterwards/then' or 'following him', and 'نیزه‌ور' is a noun-apposition 'lancer' (a spearman). Together this sequence lists figures: a mounted man standing, and then (or following) a lancer.
segments.s307-g123: 'سواران جنگی' is a compound nominal phrase 'war-riders / cavalry'. 'نگهدارشان' is a nominal/predicative form 'their keepers/guardians' (نگهدار + possessive شان). Persian allows the copula to be omitted, so the phrase reads as 'the war-riders were their guardians.'
segments.s307-g124: 'بدانگه' is a temporal adverb 'at that moment/thereupon'. The conjunction 'که' introduces the subordinate clause; 'شد' is the past tense of 'شدن' used here as 'became'/'turned (out)'; 'سخت' qualifies the following noun as 'severe/violent'; and 'پیکارشان' is 'their battle/fight' (پیکار + شان). The clause therefore means 'at that moment their battle became severe.'
segments.s307-g125: This is a coordinated subject 'سوار و پیاده' (mounted and foot), followed by the directional phrase 'به هر سو' ('to/ toward every side') and the noun 'گروه' ('troop(s)/companies'). Word order groups the actors first, then their dispersion/direction and collective description.
segments.s307-g126: 'به جنگ اندر آمد' is a verbal phrase meaning 'entered into the battle' (به ... اندر آمد = went/advanced into). 'به کردار' is a similative/instrumental phrase 'in the manner/form of', and 'کوه' is the simile 'a mountain' (i.e., en masse, like a mountain). The clause describes their entrance into battle as massive and mountain‑like.
segments.s307-g127: 'به رخنه در آورد' is a causative/verbal phrase meaning 'brought/forced into the breach' (به + noun + در آورد = to bring into/through a breach). 'یکسر سپاه' places 'یکسر' ('entirely/one whole') with 'سپاه' ('the host/army'), so the object is 'the entire army' — 'he drove the whole host into the breach.'
segments.s307-g128: 'چو' introduces a simile 'like/as'; 'شیر' is 'lion'. The element 'ژیان' is an archaic/poetic epithet here and functions adjectivally with 'شیر' (the exact lexical sense given in the list is 'life/way of life', which does not capture this poetic usage, so its precise nuance is archaic). 'رستم' is the proper name in apposition, and 'کینه‌خواه' is an epithet 'the avenger / vengeful' placed after the name. Grammatically this whole segment likens Rostam (described as vengeful) to a lion.
segments.s307-g137: بدانگه (a temporal adverb) = 'thereupon/then' placed at the clause front; کجا here functions like 'when/where' introducing the circumstance; رزمشان is رزم + -شان ('their battle/combat') and is the noun being predicated; شد is the past tense of شدن 'become' (here meaning 'became'); درشت is an adjectival predicate meaning 'rough/violent/fierce'. The whole clause means 'then, when their fighting became fierce.'
segments.s307-g138: دو تن is a numeral + noun phrase 'two persons' (تن = person/body). رستم آورد is subject + past verb 'Rostam brought/put (down)'; آورد here is a simple past transitive 'brought/caused to come'. از ایشان 'from/of them' marks the source (two of them). به مشت is an instrumental phrase 'with (his) fist' (به + noun used instrumentally). Together: 'Rostam (struck) two of them with his fist.'
segments.s307-g139: چو is the conjunction 'when'. گرسیوز و جهن are two proper names joined by و. رزم آزمای is a compound where رزم 'battle' combines with آزمای (verbal/adjectival element from آزما) meaning 'testing/trying in battle' — syntactically the predicate: 'when Garsivaz and Jahan engaged/tested themselves in battle.'
segments.s307-g140: که introduces the result/clause connected to the previous. بد is an attributive/adverbial 'bad/ill' modifying تخت (here 'ill‑fated' or 'evil'). تخت توران = 'the throne of Turan' (genitive). بدیشان is بد + ایشان literally 'bad to them / upon them' implying they were put to shame or ill‑treated; به پای is a locative phrase 'at the foot' (i.e. 'at the foot of the throne'). The clause describes them being laid low at Turan's ill‑fated throne.
segments.s307-g141: برادر یکی بود is a verbless/elliptical copular construction with بود 'was' after the noun phrase: 'there was one brother.' و links the next nominal phrase; فرخ پسر is an apposition or nominal phrase 'Farrokh (was) a son' with the copula omitted — Persian often drops the present copula in such clauses.
segments.s307-g142: چنین آمد is a fixed expression 'thus came' used impersonally = 'thus befell.' از شوربختی 'from misfortune' gives the cause/source, and به سر literally 'to the head' is an idiom meaning 'upon him' (i.e. misfortune befell him). آمد here functions like 'befell' rather than a literal 'come.'
segments.s307-g143: بدان is the fused prepositional phrase (به + آن) 'to/into that'; شارستان 'city/fortified town' is the object of the motion; اندر آمد سپاه literally 'entered came the army' — Persian allows verb before subject, so آمد (came/entered) is the verb and سپاه (army) the logical subject. The clause means 'the army entered into that city.'
segments.s307-g144: چنان 'such' introduces a quality description; داغ‌دل is a compound adjective 'dāgh‑dil' meaning 'sore‑hearted / grief‑stricken'; لشکری is an adjectival/collective form relating to 'army/troops'; کینه‌خواه 'kineh‑khāh' is a descriptive adjective 'vengeful, revenge‑seeking.' Together they form a noun phrase apposition: 'such a grief‑stricken, vengeance‑seeking host.'
segments.s307-g225: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'); کیخسرو is the proper-name subject. ایدر (اینجا) is a locative adverb 'here' placed after the subject for emphasis. بدان سان is a fixed phrase 'in that manner / so' formed here from the prefix ب- + دان + سان, and شدست is an archaic / poetic third-person perfect/predicative of شدن meaning 'has become' or 'was (made) so'. The whole segment states that Kay Khosrow 'here' had come to be in that state.
segments.s307-g226: که again introduces the comparative/completive clause 'that / so that'; گویی is a parenthetical/verbal particle meaning 'it seems / as if'. سوی is a prepositional directional 'toward/to', followed by باب 'gate/door' as its object. مهمان is a predicate noun 'a guest', and شدست is the same archaic perfect of شدن meaning 'became' or 'appeared (as)'. Together the clause means 'so that it seemed as if he had become a guest at the gate.'
segments.s307-g227: همی is an adverb of continuity 'ever/continually' placed before the predicate for emphasis. یاد is the noun 'memory / remembrance' functioning as the thematic element. نایدش is an archaic negative-form verb phrase (ناید = 'does not come' or 'does not occur') with the enclitic -ش 'to him' (i.e. 'it does not cease to come to him' / 'he keeps recalling'); خون پدر is a genitive phrase 'the blood of (his) father' that specifies what is remembered. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic emphasis.
segments.s307-g228: This is a sequence of adverbial/participial phrases introduced by به. به خیره بریده is a descriptive compound: خیره (dazed/staring) + بریده (cut off, rent) describing his stunned, torn state. The following به بیداد سر places 'سر' (head) with بیداد (injustice/cruelty), i.e. 'his head rent/struck by cruelty.' The repeated به is a poetic device introducing circumstantial phrases ('in a dazed, rent condition; his head by injustice').
segments.s307-g229: همان is the demonstrative 'that very / the same', followed by مادرش 'his mother' (مادر + enclitic ش 'his'). را is the definite object marker setting مادرش as the direct object of the relative clause that follows. که introduces the relative clause 'whom', and از تخت و گاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the throne and (inner) chamber' describing where she was taken. The whole segment sets up 'that same mother of his, whom (they took) from the throne and chamber...'.
segments.s307-g230: ز (poetic variant of از) پرده کشیدند is a past-tense clause: ز پرده 'from the curtain' + کشیدند 'they pulled/drew' (3rd plural past). یکسو is an adverb 'to one side', and به راه 'toward the road' (به as directional 'to/toward' + راه 'road/way'). The verb کشیدند is plural and the agent is implied; the phrase describes dragging her out from behind the curtain toward the road.
segments.s307-g231: شبان is 'shepherds' (plural) and پروریدست is a poetic/perfect verbal form 'was reared / has been fostered'. وز here functions as an old/poetic connective (و + ز) approximating 'and/from among', linking to گوسفند 'sheep' — together conveying 'reared by shepherds and among sheep.' The clause states his humble upbringing; verb agrees implicitly with the subject (he).
segments.s307-g232: مزیدست is a connective adverb meaning 'moreover / furthermore' introducing the next assertion. شیر is the subject 'lion', این شه is a genitive phrase 'of this king' (این = 'this', شه = 'king'), and هوشمند is an adjective 'intelligent/wise' modifying 'king'. The clause says 'moreover, the lion of this wise king ...' (the predicate 'is greater' or 'is superior' is implied or carried by مزیدست's comparative sense in the poetic context).
segments.s307-g9: شب تیره is a noun + postpositive adjective (night + dark). تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning “until.” برزد is an archaic literary verb (from برزدن / برزیدن) here meaning “rose/appeared” (3rd‑singular past/preterite sense in epic narration). از چرخ is a prepositional phrase “from the sky/heaven,” and شید is a poetic epithet placed after the prepositional phrase as the thing that 'rose' — here used for the sun/bright one.
segments.s307-g10: بشد is the narrative perfect/resultative meaning “it became/was made” (literary past). کوه is the subject 'mountain.' چون introduces a simile “like/as.” پشت پیل سپید is the simile’s ground: پشت (back) + پیل (elephant) + سپید (white), with adjective following the noun as usual; the whole phrase means “white like an elephant’s back.”
segments.s307-g11: همی is a frequent narrative particle in classical Persian that gives an ongoing or habitual shade (here a continuous/imperfective flavour: 'was (in the act of)'). لشکر آراست is verb-final order typical of Persian: subject (لشکر) + past verb آراست (arranged/arrayed). افراسیاب is the agent/name; word order places the name after the verb for poetic rhythm.
segments.s307-g12: دلش بود is a simple past copular clause (بود = 'was'); the possessive ـش attaches to دل. پردرد is a compound adjective meaning 'full of pain/sorrow' and functions as subject complement. و links the two coordinated complements; سر پر شتاب uses سر (head) + پر (postpositive 'full (of)') + شتاب (haste), a common classical order where پر follows the noun it fills.
segments.s307-g13: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when.' از introduces the source or origin of the sound ('from'). گنگ is an adjective 'muffled/indistinct' describing the sound; برخاست is the classical past verb 'rose/arose.' آوای کوس is the noun phrase 'the voice/sound of the war‑drum' (آوا + genitive/attributive کوس). The whole segment is a subordinate temporal clause.
segments.s307-g14: This is a verbless/copular verbless clause common in epic poetry: زمین آهنین شد uses زمین (earth) + adjective آهنین (iron/steely) + شد (became) — note the adjective before the copula in poetic inversion is allowed. هوا آبنوس parallels it (هوا = air/sky, آبنوس = ebony/very dark). The verbless pairing creates a dramatic image: the earth became iron, the air ebony.
segments.s307-g15: سر موبدان شاه is a genitive attribution: سر (head/chief) + موبدان (mōbedan, priests) + شاه ('of the king') — 'the chief of the king’s priests.' نیکی گمان is a compact nominal phrase meaning 'a good opinion/hope' or 'thinking well/favourably' and functions adverbially here to qualify the subject’s attitude.
segments.s307-g16: نشست is the past of نشستن 'sat/settled' used here intransitively as 'dismounted/sat down.' از بر زین is a compound prepositional phrase (از = from, بر = on/upon, زین = saddle) meaning 'from off the saddle.' سپیده‌دمان is a temporal adverbial 'at daybreak' (placed at the end for time reference).
segments.s307-g281: چه is an exclamative particle here ('what/how'); نیکو is an adjective ('fine/good') placed before the agent-noun بدی‌گر ('one who does bad / evildoer'), so the phrase is an ironic or admiring exclamation: 'what a fine evildoer'. ز + [توران زمین] is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from the land of Turan' (ز = 'from'; زمین = 'land'). Word order is adjective → agent-noun → origin phrase.
segments.s307-g282: نبودی is the past existential 'there was not / was not' (used here to state absence). به دلت اندرون is a locative phrase: به (to/into) + دلت (دل + 2nd-person suffix -ت = 'your heart') + اندرون ('within/inside'), so 'in your inner heart'. ایچ is an emphatic archaic negative 'not/none at all' and کین is the noun 'hatred, grudge' — together: 'there was not in your inner heart any hatred.'
segments.s307-g283: تو is the second-person singular subject 'you'. ایدر is an archaic/dialectal adverb 'here'. به جشن is the directional prepositional phrase 'to the feast/celebration'. و خرام links with خرام ('proud gait / proud striding') to give manner — 'and prancing' or 'with a proud stride'. آمدی is the past second-person singular 'you came'. The segment describes how/where you came.
segments.s307-g284: ز شاهان is the source phrase 'from the kings' (ز = 'from', شاهان = 'kings'). درود و پیام are coordinated nouns 'greeting(s) and message/tidings'. آمدی repeats the past 'came' (poetic repetition): syntactically it can either attach to the subject 'you' (you came bearing greetings) or be read impersonally as 'greetings and message came' — both readings are common in epic-style phrasing.
segments.s307-g285: بر این بوم و بر opens with the preposition بر ('on/upon') + این بوم ('this homeland/soil'); the repeated بر may be emphatic/poetic ('on this land and over [it]'). نیست is the existential negative 'is not / there is not'. خود is emphatic 'self/own' and کدخدای is the noun 'village headman / local chief'. The phrase says that, on this land, there is no kadhkhoda of his own (i.e. no other local chief).
segments.s307-g286: به تخت is the directional phrase 'onto the throne' (به = to/onto; تخت = throne). نیا here is an archaic element (in some contexts a prohibitive/negational or vocative), but in this line it functions within the predicate cluster with بر و نهادی; نهادی is the past-formation of نهادن ('to place, to set') and تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'; پای is 'foot'. Together the phrase means 'you did not set your foot on the throne' (poetic word order).
segments.s307-g287: سیاوش is the proper name 'Siyâvash' as subject. نگشتی is a past form of گشتن used here with the sense 'became/turned' (literally 'became' or 'you became'); the phrase به خیره uses به + خیره ('in astonishment / by staring') and تباه is a predicative adjective 'ruined / destroyed'. The clause says that Siyâvash did not become ruined by mere amazement (the phrasing is compact/poetic).
segments.s307-g288: ولیکن is the adversative conjunction 'but / yet'. چنین is an adverb 'thus / in this way'. گشت is the past third-person singular of گشتن 'became / was turned'. خورشید و ماه are the coordinated subjects 'the sun and the moon'. The clause states that (nevertheless) the sun and moon became/changed in this manner — a poetically cosmic consequence.
segments.s307-g193: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning "when" here; کیخسرو is the proper name (the legendary king) and آمد is the past tense verb "came/entered." در + ایوان is the locative phrase "into/into the iwan/porch." اوی is a possessive/enclitic here meaning "his" ("his iwan"). Word order is Subject (کیخسرو) + Verb (آمد) + location phrase (در ایوان اوی).
segments.s307-g194: به پای اندر آورد is an idiomatic sequence: به پای = "to the feet/of the foot," اندر آورد = "brought in/placed." Here آورَد is past "brought." کیوان is a proper name (the man Kiwan) and اوی is a pronominal clitic referring to the person brought (object pronoun "him"). The phrase means "(he) brought Kiwan to his feet/ before him."
segments.s307-g195: This clause has a poetic order. ابر is used metaphorically/poetically ("cloud/canopy") modifying تخت زرینش ("his golden throne"). بنشست is the past tense "sat (down)" and شاه is the subject "the king." The phrase reads roughly "The king sat on his golden throne (with a cloud-like canopy)."
segments.s307-g196: بجستنش is the noun/gerund or verbal object formed from بجستن + -ش (بجستن + اش) meaning "the seeking/search for him." بر کرد is a light-verb construction (کرد "did/made" with prefix بر) meaning "set in motion / caused to happen." هر سو سپاه literally "army to every side/direction" = "he sent the army in every direction." Together: "he set the army to search for him on every side."
