segments.s307-g197: فراوان is an adverb meaning "widely/abundantly/many (of them)." بجستند is the past plural "they searched/sought." جایی here means "anywhere/somewhere" (object of search) and نشان is "sign/trace." The clause: "They searched many places (but found) no sign/trace."
segments.s307-g198: نیامد is the past negative "did not come/appear." ز is the poetic preposition "from/of." سالار is "commander/chief" and گردنکشان is a descriptive plural "those who throw off the yoke / rebels (literally 'neck-throwers')". The clause means "no trace came from the commander of the rebels."
segments.s307-g199: ز here functions as the poetic preposition "from/about" and introduces the persons addressed: ز گرسیوز و جهن = "from/with Garsivaz and Jahan" (i.e., he asked them). پرسید is past "asked" and شاه is the subject "the king." Word order: (about) X he asked — "the king asked Garsivaz and Jahan."
segments.s307-g200: ز کار is "about the affair/action/doing" (ز = about/from, کار = affair/action). سپهدار is the title "commander" and توران specifies the domain "of Turan." سپاه is "army"; together the phrase asks about "the actions/work of the commander of Turan's army." Note that Persian often strings these nouns without additional particles.
segments.s307-g177: به دیده is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with/by (my) sight' (به + noun); بدیدم is the simple past 1st‑singular 'I saw'. همان روزگار is a noun phrase 'that very time/era' with همان emphasizing exactness and functioning as the direct complement of بدیدم.
segments.s307-g178: که introduces a subordinate clause ('when/that'). آمد is the past 3rd‑singular 'came'. The sequence م را represents the older spelling of مرا (me + object marker); here it is the object of the infinitive کشتن 'to kill', which functions as the complement of آمد ('came to kill me'). و links the following nominal phrase مرگ خوار, where خوار is an adjectival epithet ('despised'/'base') applied to مرگ; the verb is omitted by poetic ellipsis.
segments.s307-g179: پر از درد is an adjectival phrase 'full of pain' (پر + از + noun). The second از آن باره means 'from/because of that occasion' (آن باره = 'that event/instance') and modifies آمد. Note the inverted, poetic order: فرود (alighting/descent) pairs with آمد to mean 'came down/descended.'
segments.s307-g180: همی داد combines the habitual adverb همی 'continually/ever' with the past verb داد 'gave' (here 'offered' or 'bestowed'). تخت مهی را is the definite object (تخت 'throne' qualified by مهی 'moon‑like, beautiful') marked by را, and درود is the noun 'greeting' or 'salutation' as the thing given.
segments.s307-g181: همی گفت is a habitual/past phrase 'he was saying' (همی marking continuous aspect, گفت as past 'said'). کی بینمت is an interrogative clause 'when will I see you' (کی = 'when'; بینمت = 'I see you' with the enclitic object -ت). نیز باز are adverbs 'also/again' reinforcing the speaker's longing.
segments.s307-g182: ایا here is an exclamatory/introductory particle (poetic), followed by the vocative/noun phrase روز شادی و آرام و ناز 'day of joy and calm and tender grace' — three coordinated nouns linked by و describing the desirable time the speaker longs for.
segments.s307-g183: وز is an archaic connective (here 'from/then' or linking particle) used in older/poetic Persian; آن جایگه = 'that place/spot'. خیره شد is a verbal compound 'became dumbstruck/astonished' (خیره = stunned/astonished, شد = became). ناپدید is a predicate 'vanished' placed after شد as a resultant state or rapid sequence.
segments.s307-g184: تو گفتی is past 'you said' (تو = 'you', گفتی = past 2nd/3rd person form in older usage). چو مرغان is a simile 'like the birds'; همی here gives a poetic continuous/iterative flavor, and بر پرید is the past 'flew up/away' (بر combining with پرید to mean 'off/away'), so the line says 'you said (that) like the birds you flew away.'
segments.s307-g241: که is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'that' (linking this clause to what precedes). The phrase هر جای تندی = 'in any place (هر جای) haste/hastefulness (تندی)' with the impersonal modal نباید 'one must not / it should not be' followed by نمود (verbal infinitive/bare verb 'to show') — a classical Persian pattern نباید + verb = 'one should not do (verb)'. Word order is typical: adverbial (هر جای تندی) before the modal and verb.
segments.s307-g242: سر بی‌خرد را is the definite/object phrase ('the head of the fool' — idiomatically 'the foolish person') with را marking the direct object. نشاید is impersonal 'it is not fitting/should not be' and is followed by ستود (bare verb 'to praise') as its verbal complement — together meaning 'it is not fitting to praise (him)'. Note the classical sequence: object + نشاید + verb.
segments.s307-g243: همان به که is an idiomatic formulation meaning roughly 'it is best/appropriate that'. The preposition به here is part of the fixed phrase, که introduces the subordinate clause. با کینه 'with grudge/hatred' modifies داد آوریم: داد (here 'justice' / 'vengeance') is the object/idea and آوریم is first-person-plural subjunctive/present ('we bring/perform'); together داد آوریم = 'we exact/bring about justice/vengeance.'
segments.s307-g244: به کام اندرون is a purposive/benefactive phrase 'to the satisfaction of the inner self' (به + کام + اندرون). نام یاد آوریم uses نام 'name' and یاد 'remembrance' with آوریم ('we bring/recall') — a verbal phrase meaning 'let us bring the name to remembrance' or 'let us recall his name' to satisfy our inward desire. آوریم is again the first-person-plural verbal form, here hortative/subjunctive ('let us').
segments.s307-g245: که introduces an explanatory clause. نیکیست is an impersonal/categorical copular phrase 'goodness/good deed exists' or here better read as 'good deed' in the general statement. اندر جهان 'in the world' is the locative phrase, and یادگار (postposed) is the noun 'memorial/memento' describing how a good deed might remain; the whole sets up the claim about the fate of good deeds in the world.
segments.s307-g246: نماند is the negative/limited-present form 'does not remain / does not endure'. The phrase نماند به کس = 'does not remain for anyone', and جاودان روزگار is a genitival/attributive phrase 'eternal time' meaning 'eternity' or 'forever' — together: 'does not remain as an eternal thing for anyone.' Word order places the negating verb before the beneficiary (به کس) and the qualifier (جاودان روزگار) at the end.
segments.s307-g247: همین چرخ گردنده is the noun phrase 'this very turning wheel' (همین emphasises 'this/very', چرخ گردنده = 'rotating wheel' i.e. the wheel of fortune). با هر کسی uses با + هر کسی to indicate 'upon/against anyone' (the wheel acts with regard to any person). The phrase sets up the subject/agent that will act in the following segment.
segments.s307-g248: تواند is the modal 'can / is able to' (third person singular). جفا is the object 'cruelty / injustice', followed by گستریدن, the infinitive/masdar-form 'to spread / to inflict' (here functioning as the verbal complement of تواند: 'can inflict'). بسی is an adverb meaning 'greatly / much / in abundance' modifying گستریدن. The classical order places the object before the infinitive complement.
segments.s307-g33: همه گرد آن شارستان is an adverbial phrase: همه گرد = 'all around' (همه + گرد) and آن شارستان = 'that city/town' (demonstrative + noun). چون نوند is a simile ('like Navand'); the simile phrase sits immediately after the locative phrase and sets the manner in which they moved, preparing for the verb that follows.
segments.s307-g34: بگشتند و جستند is a compound of two simple past verbs (3rd pl. b-a-gasht-and, they turned/returned; jost-and, they sought/searched) joined by و 'and'. هر گونه بند is the object phrase placed after the verbs: هر گونه = 'every kind/type of' modifies بند ('fetter/fastening/restraint') — i.e. 'they searched every kind of fastening'.
segments.s307-g35: دو نیزه = 'two spears' (numeral + noun). به بالا is an adverbial phrase 'upward/aloft' (به + بالا). یکی کنده کرد is a verbal phrase: کنده (past participle 'detached/uprooted') + کرد (simple past auxiliary 'did/caused') = 'he tore off/detached one' (i.e. pulled one free and raised it).
segments.s307-g36: سپه را is the definite object marked by را ('the army' as direct object). به گردش here is a purposive/causal adverbial 'into a turning or rout' (به + گردش). پراگنده کرد is a causative past ('made/scattered' = caused to be scattered); the verb کرد takes سپه را as its object: 'he caused the army to be scattered.'
segments.s307-g37: بدان is an older compounded/deictic/adverbial form (ب‑ + دان) used here to point back/mark consequence ('because of that' / 'to that point'); تا introduces a temporal limit 'until'. شب تیره = 'the dark/black night' (adjective after noun is poetic order). بی ساختن is 'without making' (بی + infinitive ساختن) — a negative adjunct modifying the following action.
segments.s307-g38: نیارند here functions as an irregular/archaic negative/modal verb form meaning 'they did not dare / did not attempt' (classical usage; the lexicon given doesn't supply an exact match). ترکان is the subject 'the Turks'. یکی تاختن is an infinitival phrase 'to make one charge' (یکی modifies the infinitive تاختن). The whole clause thus means 'the Turks did not attempt even a single charge.'
segments.s307-g39: دو صد is the numeral 'two hundred' preceding the noun. ساخت here is used as a past/nominalized form 'made/constructed' modifying عراده (military wagon); the phrase بر هر دری (classical word order) means 'at/on each gate' (بر + هر + در). Note: دری here is the orthography used in the edition for 'در' (gate/door).
segments.s307-g40: دو صد منجنیق = 'two hundred catapults' (numeral + siege engines). از پس لشکری is a locative/relational phrase 'from/after (i.e. following) the army' (از پس = 'behind/after'), so the clause places the catapults behind the troops.
segments.s307-g209: ز + noun begins a prepositional phrase 'from the army'. گزین کرد is a compound verbal expression: گزین (choose) + کرد (did) = 'chose' in the past. پس is a temporal adverb 'then'. بخردان is the plural noun 'the wise (men)' and here functions as the object of selection (the indirect/accusative target); word order is prepositional phrase → verb → adverb → object for poetic emphasis.
segments.s307-g210: This is a noun phrase in which two adjectives/epithets (جهاندیده 'world-wise' and کاربین 'experienced/observer of deeds') are joined by و 'and' and modify موبدان, the plural of موبد (mōbed, Zoroastrian priest). Adjectives precede the head noun here for poetic effect; the whole phrase names the people chosen above.
segments.s307-g211: بدیشان = بدی + شان, a dative phrase 'to them'. چنین گفت = 'said thus' (چنین adverb + گفت past of 'to say'). کآباد here is a proper name (Kâbâd) introduced as the subject of the short wish/clause that follows; بید is an archaic/poetic present/jussive meaning 'be awake' or 'may (he) be awake' — an optative/formal present used to express a wish.
segments.s307-g212: همیشه 'always' is an adverb modifying the following prepositional phrase. به هر کار literally 'to every affair' functions idiomatically as 'in every matter'. با داد 'with justice' is an instrumental phrase; the final بید repeats the archaic optative/present 'be awake' — together the clause is a jussive wish: 'May he always, in every affair, be with justice (i.e. remain just/awakened to justice).'
segments.s307-g213: در گنج is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the treasury/vault'. این ترک is 'this Turk' and شوریده بخت is an epithet made of شوریده (distraught/mad) + بخت (fortune/fate), forming a post‑nominal compound adjective 'ill‑fated / distraught of fortune' that modifies ترک. Word order places the descriptive phrase immediately after the noun.
segments.s307-g214: شما را سپردم is 'I entrusted (it/him) to you' — را marks the definite object/recipient (here pronounally used with شما). سپردم is the past tense 'I entrusted/handed over'. بکوشید is a 2nd‑person plural/formal present-subjunctive/imperative 'strive' or 'make an effort', and سخت is an adverb 'hard/firmly' modifying بکوشید.
segments.s307-g215: نباید is an impersonal modal 'it must not / one must not' and introduces a subordinate clause with که 'that'. بر کاخ افراسیاب = 'upon Afrasiab's palace' (بر + noun), with افراسیاب a proper name; the subordinate clause that follows gives what must not happen.
segments.s307-g216: بتابد is the present/jussive form of تابیدن 'to shine' (here 'shine/upon'); ز is the ablative/poetic preposition 'from' and introduces چرخ بلند آفتاب, an inverted poetic order meaning 'the sun of the high/lofty sky' (چرخ 'heaven/sky' modified by بلند). The whole phrase yields 'that the lofty sun of heaven shine upon ...' with the verb placed before the expanded subject for poetic rhythm.
segments.s307-g305: چنین modifies the following noun phrase زندگانی and means “such.” زندگانی is the noun “life.” همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (classical/poetic) reinforcing or continualizing the statement (here best rendered “indeed” or “truly”). مرگ is the predicate noun and اوست is the copular phrase (او = ‘he’, ست = older copula ‘is’), so literal order is “such a life indeed death his is” → “Such a life is indeed his death.”
segments.s307-g306: شگفت introduces a comment of wonder (“it is strange/wonderful that…”). آن‌که is a relative/nominalizer meaning “that which/that (which).” بر تن is a prepositional phrase “on (his) body.” ندردش is a negative verbal form with the enclitic ـش referring back to the person/thing involved; in classical usage here it functions as a negative present/optative sense ‘does not tear/come off’ (poetic usage where دریدن/دَرَدن can mean ‘to tear off’). پوست is the noun ‘skin’ and (in the word order) is thematically linked as the thing concerned by ندردش (“that the skin does not tear from the body”).
segments.s307-g307: کنون is the temporal adverb “now.” از پی is a prepositional phrase here meaning roughly “for the sake of/on account of” or “in relation to” (classical idiom often = ‘in pursuit of/for the sake of’); it governs بیگناهان, ‘the innocent.’ به ما is a dative phrase “to/for us.” The whole segment sets the frame: “Now, on account of the innocent, toward us…”
segments.s307-g308: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (a verbal noun/command root meaning ‘look/keep watch’) plus the imperative particle کن ‘do!’ = “look!” or “keep watch!” بر آیین شاهان is a prepositional phrase where بر + آیین means “according to / upon the custom” and شاهان is the genitive/possessive ‘of kings.’ به ما repeats the dative “toward us/for us” — so the clause is a command: “Look to us; observe toward us the kings’ custom” → “Treat/consider us by the ways of kings.”
segments.s307-g309: همه is a universal quantifier “all/everyone.” پاک is an adjective “pure.” پیوسته is an adverb meaning “constantly/ever.” خسرویم is the present copular verb with first‑person plural ending (خسرو + ـیم) meaning “we are kings/sovereigns.” Word order places modifiers before the predicate: “All pure, constantly, we are sovereigns.”
segments.s307-g310: جز از is the exception marker “except (from).” نام او is the genitive phrase “his name.” در جهان is the locative “in the world.” نشنویم is the first‑person plural negative present/subjunctive form (ن + شنَو + ایم) meaning “we do not hear / we hear not.” Together: “Except that we do not hear his name in the world.”
segments.s307-g311: به here introduces the manner/cause “by/through.” بد کردن جادو is a light‑verb construction where کردن ‘to do/make’ attaches to the noun جادو ‘magic’ and بد qualifies it: “doing evil magic” or “practising evil sorcery.” افراسیاب follows as the agent; classical Persian often places the agent after the instrumental phrase so the whole chunk reads “Afrasiab, by doing evil magic…”
segments.s307-g312: نگیرد is a third‑person present negative form (ن + گیرد) that here has a jussive/optative force “may he not take/let him not seize.” بر introduces the target “upon / against.” این بیگناهان is the object phrase “these innocents.” شتاب is a noun “haste/urgency” placed after the object; together the clause means roughly “may he not, in haste, fall upon/seize these innocents.”
segments.s307-g353: ز introduces the source or point of removal ('from'); دلها is the plural substantive 'hearts' (دل + ها). همه کینه is the direct object 'all hatred/grudges' placed before the verb for emphasis. کنید is the 2nd-person plural imperative/formal present of کردن used here as the command 'do/drive out.'
segments.s307-g354: به مهر is an instrumental/adverbial phrase 'with/in/by love' (به + noun). اندر این کشور is a locative phrase 'in this country' using the archaic/pre-classical اندر. افسون کردن is a verbal expression 'to enchant/charm' with کنید again the 2nd-person plural imperative 'do/enchant,' so the clause means 'make/enspell this land with affection.'
segments.s307-g355: که here introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/because'). از ما means 'on our account' or 'because of us.' چنین دردشان places the noun 'such a pain/sorrow' with the possessive enclitic -شان 'their' before the predicate. در دلست is an older, contracted copular expression (دل + است → دلست) meaning 'is in the heart,' so the whole clause: 'for because of us such sorrow is in their hearts.'
segments.s307-g356: ز خون ریختن is a nominal/gerundial construction 'from/the shedding of blood' (ز + verbal noun رِیختن). گرد کشور is a locative/adverbial 'around/about the country.' گلست is a poetic contraction (گل است) meaning 'is flowery / is like flowers' or 'is adorned' — here used predicatively to describe the country as made (grimly) flower-like by bloodshed.
segments.s307-g357: همه گنج توران is the noun phrase 'all the treasure(s) of Turan' placed before the recipient. شما را uses را as a marker with the second-person plural pronoun to indicate the beneficiaries ('to you'); Persian often places the object/beneficiary before the verb. (The verb دهم 'I give' is understood as the speaker's promise.)
segments.s307-g358: بر آن گنج دادن treats دادن as a verbal noun 'the giving of that treasure' with بر آن marking 'upon/on that.' سپاهی appears as the (direct) object 'an army/men-at-arms' and نهم is an archaic 1st-person present/subjunctive form ('I will set/place/give'), so the clause says 'for that giving I will set an army.'
segments.s307-g359: بکوشید is the 2nd-person plural imperative/formal 'strive' or 'do your best.' The conjunction و links to the next clause. خوبی به کار آوردن is an idiom 'to employ/apply goodness' where به کار is 'into practice' and آورید is the 2nd-person plural imperative 'bring/cause,' so the whole means 'strive and make goodness operative / put good deeds to work.'
segments.s307-g360: چو here is the temporal 'when.' دیدند is a past/pluperfect-form used in temporal clauses ('when they/you see' in older usage). سرما 'winter' is the temporal noun, and بهار آورید is an imperative clause 'bring spring' where آورید is again 2nd-person plural. Taken together: 'When (you/they) see winter, bring spring.'
segments.s307-g369: ز + noun phrase (ز چیز کسان) = 'from / because of the things of people'; سر بتابید is an instruction with بتابید functioning as a 2nd‑person plural/polite imperative 'turn (your) head'. نیز placed after the verb means 'also' or 'too'. The whole segment = 'also turn your head away from people's things.'
segments.s307-g370: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause ('for / because'); the clause uses an inverted order د‌شمن شود دوست where دشمن (predicate noun) precedes the verb شود and دوست is the subject — a common poetic/topical inversion meaning 'the friend becomes an enemy.' شود here has an optative/indicative sense 'may become'. از بهر چیز = 'for the sake of a thing' (از بهر = 'because of / for').
segments.s307-g371: نیاید is a negative present/impersonal form meaning 'it must not / it does not become'; جهان‌آفرین را پسند literally 'the Creator's approval' (را marks definiteness/object), so the phrase means 'it is not pleasing to the Creator' or 'the Creator would not approve.'
segments.s307-g372: که introduces the reason ('that / because'); جوینده is the agent 'the seeker'; بر + بیگناهان = 'upon the innocent'; گزند is a noun 'harm' used predicatively (poetic ellipsis: 'to bring harm'), so the clause means 'that a seeker should bring harm upon the innocent.'
segments.s307-g373: هر آن کس که is the standard relative 'whoever / any person who'; جوید is a present/habitual verb 'seeks'; همی is the classical adverbial particle marking ongoing or immediate action; رای من = 'my counsel/opinion' (رای used as 'counsel/plan').
segments.s307-g374: نباید is an impersonal modal 'must not / should not'; که introduces the subordinate clause; ویران کند جای من is a verbal clause with کند ('do/make') and ویران as the action 'to ruin/destroy' and جای من = 'my place/seat' as the object — 'he must not destroy my place.'
segments.s307-g375: و دیگر که خوانند = 'and others who call' (که here is a relative/complementizer); خوانند is 3rd‑person plural 'they call' and the following بیداد و شوم are the epithets applied ('bīdād' = 'injustice/oppressor', شوم here is an adjective 'ill‑omened / unlucky'). The subject 'they' is implicit.
segments.s307-g376: که here introduces the relative clause 'who / that'; ویران کند = 'may destroy' (ویران used with کند to mean 'to lay waste'); مهتر آباد بوم strings three nouns/adjectives: مهتر 'chief/headman', آباد 'prosperous/inhabited', بوم 'land/homeland' — together 'the chief's prosperous homeland' (poetic noun grouping where آباد qualifies بوم).
segments.s307-g249: از آن پس is an adverbial phrase 'from that time on' (از = 'from', آن = 'that', پس = 'after'). بفرمود is a past 3rd-person honorific verb 'he ordered/said' (classical form with the prefixed ب-); شاه جهان is the title 'king of the world' (title following the verb—typical inverted/poetic order).
segments.s307-g250: که introduces a subordinate content clause ('that'). آرند is a 3rd-person plural present/jussive (classical) form of آوردن used after a command to mean 'let them bring / that they bring'. پوشیدگان را is the definite object (پوشیدگان 'the veiled/covered ones', را is the object marker). نهان is a postposed adjective/adverb 'hidden' or 'in secret', completing the command 'bring ... hidden'.
segments.s307-g251: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. ایرانیان is the subject 'the Iranians' and آگهی یافتند is a compound predicate: آگهی (news/information) comes before the verb یافتند (past 3rd plural of یافتن 'they found/received'), so the clause means 'they received/learned the news'.
segments.s307-g252: پر از کین is a pre-verbal adjectival/adverbial phrase 'full of hatred' (پر 'full', از 'of', کین 'hatred/vengeance'). سوی کاخ is the directional phrase 'toward the palace' (سوی = 'towards', کاخ = 'palace'). بشتافتند is past 3rd-person plural 'they hurried/rushed', so together 'full of hatred they rushed toward the palace.'
segments.s307-g253: بر آن گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in that manner' or 'thus' (بر = 'in/on', آن گونه = 'that way/manner'). بردند is past 3rd-person plural of بردن used here in a mental sense 'they took/held/entertained'. گردان گمان ('a host/crowd of suspicions') is a metaphorical object: گردان (literally 'troop/host') modifies گمان ('suspicion/conjecture'), so 'they entertained many suspicions.'
segments.s307-g254: که introduces the content of their suspicion ('that ...'). خسرو is the king (here a title/name). سرآرد is a classical 3rd-person singular form used after verbs of thought/gambling on possibility; here it expresses the notion 'might come / might befall' (subjunctive/optative sense). بر ایشان زمان literally 'time upon them' indicates the idea 'that the moment/time for Khosrow to appear/be upon them had come' — i.e. they feared Khosrow would come upon them.
segments.s307-g255: به خواری is a verbal complement 'to humiliation' (به = 'to/for', خواری = 'humiliation'). همی is a classical/perhaps frequentative/emphatic particle placed before the verb to give an ongoing or emphatic tone. نزدشان 'near them / in their presence' marks the target; خواستند is past 3rd-person plural 'they wanted/desired' — together 'they wanted to humiliate them.'
segments.s307-g256: به تاراج و کشتن is a purposive phrase 'for plunder and killing' (به = 'for/to', تاراج = 'pillage', و = 'and', کشتن = 'killing'). بیاراستند is past 3rd-person plural of آوردن in the sense 'they arrayed/adorned themselves' used figuratively here to mean 'they readied/equipped themselves' for those deeds.
segments.s307-g25: سپه را: 'سپه' (army) takes the object-marker 'را' here to mark the definite/affected group ('the army'). 'همه' follows to emphasize entirety ('the whole army'). 'هرچه بایست' is a free relative (‘whatever was necessary’) that precedes the noun it qualifies, and 'ساز' is a noun meaning 'means/equipment' (not a musical instrument) — so the phrase as a whole is a noun phrase: 'the whole army, whatever equipment was needed'.
segments.s307-g26: بکرد is simple past ('he did/made') and completes the verbal idea begun by the previous noun phrase (i.e. 'he made/prepared [the equipment]'). 'و بیامد' ('and he came') links the next action; بیامد is past tense 'came'. 'بر دژ فراز' is a prepositional phrase: بر + goal 'to/toward' the 'دژ' (fortress) qualified by 'فراز' (on high), so 'to the high citadel.'
segments.s307-g27: به لشکر bفرمود is an order construction: 'به لشکر' ('to/for the army') is the indirect object, and بفرمود is the honorific past of 'to command/say' ('he ordered/commanded'). 'پس شهریار' ('then the king') is a temporal/agent phrase placed after the verb by poetic inversion (classical word order is flexible).
segments.s307-g28: یکی کنده کردن is an infinitival phrase specifying the commanded action: 'یکی' here is indefinite ('a certain/someone' or 'one [person/action]'), 'کنده' is the participial/derived form from کندن (to dig) and together with کردن forms the verbal idea 'to dig' (کردن here as light/auxiliary verb). 'به گرد حصار' is a prepositional phrase 'around the rampart/fortification' specifying where to dig.
segments.s307-g29: بدان کار = به آن کار ('to that work') with ب (prepositional prefix) + دان (classical/demonstrative element) + کار ('task'). 'هر کس که دانا' is a relative clause 'every person who was knowledgeable/skillful'. بدند is a past plural form ('they were' / 'they gave/they set' in classical usage) and functions as the main verb tying the assignment to the people named.
segments.s307-g30: به جنگ دژ اندر توانا بدند lays out the second purpose/place: 'به جنگ دژ' ('in the battle at the fortress'), 'اندر' is a locative preposition ('in/within'), 'توانا' is an adjective 'capable/able'. The final بدند is the same past plural verb; syntactically the clause means they 'were (made) capable for the fortress fight' or they assigned capable men to the fortress combat.
segments.s307-g31: چه ... چه ... is a correlative/exhaustive listing ('whether ... or ...'). 'از چین وز روم وز هندوان' enumerates origins: 'از' = 'from', 'چین' = China, 'روم' = Rome/Byzantium, 'هندوان' = the Indians; 'وز' is a poetic/archaic conjunction (here functioning like و 'and') used between items.
segments.s307-g32: چه رزم آزموده ز هر سو گوان: 'رزم آزموده' is a compound adjective 'battle‑tested' (رزم + past participle آزموده). 'ز هر سو' = 'from every side/quarter', and 'گوان' is an adverb meaning 'alike/so' — together: 'they were battle‑tested from every quarter.'
segments.s307-g89: ز here is the classical preposition 'from/with' introducing the material used. نفط سیه is a noun + adjective ('black oil' or pitch), چوبها is the plural 'the timbers/woods' (چوب + plural -ها), and برفروخت is a past action verb meaning 'was kindled/set on fire' (literary prefix بـ + سوخت). The phrase literally says 'with/from black oil the timbers were kindled.'
segments.s307-g90: به فرمان یزدان is an ablative/agent phrase 'by the command of God' (به used here as 'by' + فرمان 'command' + یزدان 'God'). چو هیزم بسوخت is a simile: چو = 'as/like', هیزم = 'firewood', and بسوخت (past) = 'was burned' or 'burned up'. The clause says the wood burned as if at God's command.
segments.s307-g91: نگون باره is an adverbial phrase meaning 'ill‑omened' or 'doom‑laden' (نگون + باره, where باره here = 'instance/utterance'). گفتی is the second‑person past 'you said', and که introduces the reported clause. برداشت پای is verb + object (برداشت, past 'he lifted/took up' + پای 'foot')—literary word order places the verb before the object.
segments.s307-g92: به کردار کوه is a comparative/figurative phrase 'in the manner/form of a mountain' (به + کردار 'manner/way' + کوه 'mountain'). اندر آمد ز جای literally 'came forth from his place' (اندر آمد = 'came in/entered/appeared', ز = 'from', جای = 'place'). Together it describes a person stepping out as if like a mountain.
segments.s307-g93: وز آن باره is a classical/prepositional phrase meaning 'from that event/occurrence' (وز here is an archaic variant of ز). چندی ز ترکان دلیر is partitive: چندی = 'some/several', ز = 'of/from', ترکان دلیر = 'the brave Turks' (noun + adjective). So the segment says 'several of those brave Turks (from) that occurrence...'.
segments.s307-g94: نگون اندر آمد combines نگون 'overthrown/downfallen' with اندر آمد 'came/fell in', giving the sense 'were overthrown/fell down'. چو باران به زیر is a simile: چو = 'like', باران = 'rain', and به زیر = 'to the ground/beneath' (به + زیر). The whole image is 'they fell to the ground like rain.'
segments.s307-g95: که introduces a relative/conditional clause ('who/that'). آید is the present/habitual 'comes' (here 'falls into'), and به دام اندرون means 'into the inner trap' (به = 'into/towards', دام = 'trap', اندرون = 'inside/within'). ناگهان is the adverb 'suddenly' modifying the verb.
segments.s307-g96: سر آرد is a literary idiom: سر (head) precedes the verb آرد ('brings'), together meaning 'to bring upon one's head' or 'to incur'. بر آن شوربختی جهان = 'upon that misfortune of the world' (بر = 'upon', آن = 'that', شوربختی = 'misfortune', جهان = 'world'). The clause therefore means 'brings upon his head the world's misfortune.'
segments.s307-g65: This is a noun phrase: ابر خاک is a compound meaning 'a cloud of dust' (ابَر 'cloud' + خاک 'dust'). چون مار پیچان is a simile placed after the noun ('چون' = 'like'), with پیچان 'twisting' qualifying the cloud. ز introduces the cause ('from/with'); کین 'vengeance/hatred' gives the reason or character of that twisting cloud.
segments.s307-g66: همی + verb gives a continuous/habitual sense in epic Persian ('kept doing' / 'was continually doing'). خواند here is the verbal action 'sang/uttered/called' in that ongoing sense. بر + noun marks direction/target ('toward the Creator'), and آفرین functions as an exclamation/noun 'praise, acclamation'.
segments.s307-g67: همی گفت is again habitual/continuous speech ('he kept saying'). کام و بلندی is a coordinated noun phrase 'desire and exaltation' (کام = wish/desire; بلندی = loftiness/exaltation). ز تست marks origin/source ('are from you') — تست here is the archaic/poetic form referring to 'you' or 'your doing' (note: the supplied lemma senses do not reflect this personal meaning).
segments.s307-g68: به هر سختیی sets the frame 'in/at every hardship' (به = in/at; هر = every; سختیی = hardship, an archaic/poetic form). یارمندی is the favor/assistance that comes in such times; ز تست again marks that this help originates 'from you'. The clause as a whole repeats the attribution of all good things to the addressee.
segments.s307-g69: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). داد بینی is literally 'you see/consider as just' (داد = justice; بینی = 'you see' / 'you deem'), so the clause means 'if you find [it] just'. همی here intensifies the utterance/continuity; رای من 'my counsel/opinion' is the item being judged. Word order places the noun judged (داد) before the verb بینی, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s307-g70: مگردان is a negative imperative 'do not turn away / do not remove'. از + این جایگه 'from this place' gives the source/location. پای من ('my foot') is a possessive noun phrase meaning literally 'my foot' but idiomatically 'my footing / my presence' — 'do not make me leave this spot.'
segments.s307-g71: نگون کن is a causative/imperative construction: نگون ('overthrown' / 'down') + کن (imperative 'do') = 'bring down/overthrow'. سر جادوان را is the definite object (را marks it): 'the heads of the magicians' or 'the magicians, head(s)'. ز تخت is a prepositional phrase 'from the throne' indicating the source or place from which they should be removed.
segments.s307-g72: مرا دار is an imperative request with مرا ('me' as object) + دار (here 'hold/keep' in the sense 'let me remain' or 'grant me'); the supplied segmentation in the word list is irregular, but the phrase functions as 'keep me'. شادان‌دل و نیک‌بخت are two adjectives in coordination 'cheerful/joyful‑hearted and fortunate', describing the desired state to be kept for the speaker.
segments.s307-g385: همی is the classical progressive/iterative particle (here ≈ 'kept' / continuous aspect). داد is the past tense verb 'gave' (here 'gave/sounded'). زنهار is a warning/cry ('a warning' or the interjection 'beware'). و is the coordinating 'and'. بنواختشان is a past tense verb+noun-suffix: بنواخت (struck/sounded) with the object suffix -شان 'them' (here 'struck/sounded them').
segments.s307-g386: به زودی is an adverbial phrase 'soon' (به used to form an adverbial phrase + زودی 'soon'). همی repeats the iterative/progressive particle. کار here means 'the affair / the deed / the matter' and بر ساختشان is a verbal phrase: بر + ساخت (past of ساختن 'to make/do') with the enclitic -شان 'them' — together 'brought the matter about / accomplished it'.
segments.s307-g387: سران 'the chiefs/leaders' is the definite/accusative noun; را is the object/definiteness marker attached after سران. ز here is the poetic preposition 'from / in (of)' introducing the source/location (ز توران زمین = 'the land of Turan'). بهر داد is 'gave as a grant/for the purpose of' (بهر here marks the grant/for the benefit of, followed by داد 'gave').
segments.s307-g388: به هر = 'to each / to every'; نامداری here refers to distinguished persons/notables (contextually 'each notable'). یکی شهر داد: یکی 'one/a single', شهر 'city', داد 'gave' — 'he gave a city to each notable.' Word order is typical Persian: indirect object phrase (به هر نامداری) + direct object (یکی شهر) + verb (داد).
segments.s307-g389: به هر کشوری = 'in every country / in each province' (به used locatively). هر که = 'whoever' (the second هر used as indefinite with که). فرمان نبرد: فرمان + نبرد — here the verbal construction corresponds to 'obey the command' (فرمان بردن); the form نبرد is classical and negative/optative in shape in older Persian, but the lemma senses listed do not match this verbal use, so that sense is not among the provided options.
segments.s307-g390: ز دست = 'by the hand(s) of' (agent expressed with ز + دست). دلیران او = 'his brave ones' (دلیران plural 'brave men' with possessor او 'his'). جان نبرد here means 'took life' / 'caused to lose life' — again نبرد is the same classical-form verb used in a sense not listed in the provided lemma senses, so the provided noun/sense options do not match and are set to -1.
segments.s307-g391: شدند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they became' / 'they were made'. آن زمان = 'that time' ('at that time' — آن here used deictically). شاه را + چاکران is a classical Persian word order: X را Y شدند = 'they became Y of X' — so 'they became the king's servants.' را is the object/definiteness marker after شاه, and چاکران is the predicate noun 'servants/devotees'.
segments.s307-g392: چو = 'when'. پیوسته شد is a compound where پیوسته (connected/joined/collected) + شد 'became/was' gives 'were joined / arrived together' (i.e. the letters came in together). نامه مهتران = 'the letters of the chiefs' (مهتران here is the plural of مهتر 'chiefs/great ones' — the provided lemma senses list is not ideal for this nominal, so the closest sense index is chosen from that list).
segments.s307-g17: بیامد is a third-person past-tense narrative verb ('he came'). بگردید گرد حصار is a contiguous action: بگردید (here a past narrative/iterative form built with the verbal prefix بـ + گردیدن) + گرد 'around' + حصار 'rampart/fortress'. The sequence shows two successive actions: he came and then went about/circled the rampart.
segments.s307-g18: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: نگه 'a watch/gaze' + کرد (past 'did/made') = 'kept watch'. تا here introduces purpose/result ('so that / in order that'). چون is the interrogative/comparative 'how', and کند is the present/subjunctive form 'should do' (classical use after تا to indicate intended/future action). کارزار is the object 'battle'. Together: 'he kept watch in order to see how he should conduct the battle.'
segments.s307-g19: به رستم بفرمود is 'he said/commanded to Rostam'—بفرمود is an honorific/past reporting verb used for issuing orders. تا introduces the subordinate purpose clause 'so that / in order that'. همچو کوه is a simile 'like a mountain' modifying what Rostam is to bring or form.
segments.s307-g20: بیارد is the verb of the تا-clause meaning 'bring/lead (may he bring)' — a subjunctive/future-oriented form expected after تا. Word order is verb -> goal/adverbial: بیارد (bring) + به یک‌سو 'to one side' + ز دریا گروه 'a troop/group from the sea'. Note: the supplied sense list for بیارد does not match this verbal use, so its sense is marked unknown below.
segments.s307-g21: دگر = 'another/next'. سوش is an older form of سو (toward/side) marking the side/direction. گستهم here is a proper name (Gostahem); syntactically it is the person assigned to that side. نوذر is another proper name (Nowzar). به پای ('to the foot/of the base') indicates position—they are placed at the foot/base of that side. Note: the supplied sense for گستهم does not reflect the proper-name use, so it is flagged below.
segments.s307-g22: سه دیگر = 'three others' (numeral + 'other'). چو = 'like/as' introducing a comparison to گودرز (Goudarz). فرخنده رای is a two-word attributive phrase: فرخنده 'auspicious/fortunate' modifying رای 'counsel/opinion' — 'of auspicious counsel'. Word order places the adjective before رای to form a descriptive epithet.
segments.s307-g23: به سوی = 'toward/to the side of'. چهارم is the ordinal 'fourth' (here indicating the fourth side). شه نامدار = 'the famed/renowned king' (شه 'king' + نامدار 'renowned'); the adjective follows the noun in this poetic ordering.
segments.s307-g24: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing military elements assigned there: کوس 'war-drum(s)', پیلان (plural of پیل) 'elephants', چندی سوار 'several horsemen'. ابا appears before the list; in the poem it functions to introduce/assign these elements to that side (an archaic or context-specific form), so the line reads as '(he) set/assigned ... drums and elephants and some horsemen.' The nouns are simply enumerated with و 'and'. The supplied sense for ابا does not match this usage and is therefore flagged unknown below.
segments.s307-g57: This is a prepositional/locative phrase introduced by به + phrase: به (here 'towards/into') + زیر اندرون ('under/into the interior'). The list that follows (آتش و نفط و چوب) are coordinated direct objects/items placed into that interior; نفط here is the old word for lamp‑oil rather than modern petroleum. Word order is preposition → spatial noun → coordinated goods.
segments.s307-g58: ز بر is a poetic instrumental/location-marking sequence ('from/on/against'); here it introduces the means or action that follows. گرزهای گران is the noun phrase 'heavy maces' (گرز + plural suffix + adjective گران). The reduplicated کوب کوب is a frequentative/adverbial or verbal echo meaning 'striking/pounding again and again' (the verb is implied or represented by this echo).
segments.s307-g59: به هر چارسو is an adverbial phrase 'on/at every quarter/direction' (به here marking location). ساخت is a past tense verb 'built/made'; آن کارزار is the direct object 'that battlefield' (آن = 'that' making it definite). The verb follows the locative phrase, a normal word order in Persian poetry.
segments.s307-g60: چنانچون بود is a simile clause 'so/just as it was' (چنانچون = 'as/just as', بود = past of 'to be'). ساز here is used in the meaning 'structure/making' (not musical instrument), followed by جنگ حصار 'of war/war‑fort' (poetic genitive relation). The overall phrase says the battlefield was like the construction/works of a fortified war‑camp.
segments.s307-g61: وزآن is a fused conjunction/preposition 'and/from that' linking back to the prior place; جایگه is 'place/position'. شهریار زمین is an epithet 'the king/sovereign of the land' (noun + attributive noun). This whole segment supplies the subject (the king) and its originating place.
segments.s307-g62: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. به پیش is a prepositional pair 'before/in the presence of' and جهان‌آفرین is a title for God, 'the World‑Creator'. Subject (شهریار زمین) appears in the previous segment and the verb here completes the clause.
segments.s307-g63: ز لشکر بشد is a short, idiomatic clause 'he withdrew/left from the army' (ز = 'from', لشکر = 'army', بشد = past/intransitive 'it happened / he became' used here to indicate the action of withdrawing). تا introduces purpose/sequence 'so that / in order to' and به جای نماز = 'to the place of prayer' (destination).
segments.s307-g64: ابا here functions as a noun/adverbial 'reluctance' or 'with reluctance' (poetic usage). کردگار جهان is the indirect object 'to the Creator of the world' (the dative 'to' is expressed here without an explicit به, a common poetic economy). گفت is the past verb 'said/told' and راز is the direct object 'the secret' placed after the verb. The sequence thus means 'with reluctance he told the World‑Creator the secret.'
segments.s307-g265: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خورشید تابان is a noun phrase (lit. 'the shining sun') used metaphorically for the most beautiful woman; از ایشان ('from among them') gives the source of that figure. گَهَر (gohar) here is the direct object (a jewel) that will be linked to the verb in the next segment; the object is fronted in the line's poetic word order.
segments.s307-g266: به پیش اندر افگنده combines the prepositional phrase به پیش 'before/in the presence of' with the perfective verb افگنده 'cast/let fall' (classical past form). افگنده governs the jewel mentioned earlier; the phrase از شرم سر is causal/resultative: '(they) (lowered) the head from shame' — the verb is understood to extend to 'سر' by poetic ellipsis (the lowering of heads is implied). Note the inverted/elliptical order typical of epic verse.
segments.s307-g267: به یک دست مجمر به یک دست جام is a paired instrumental/location structure: به + numeral + دست 'with/in one hand' followed by the object held (مجمر, a brazier/censer), then the parallel phrase for the other hand. Here به marks the instrument/position and the two noun phrases stand in parallel (typical parataxis in Persian poetry).
segments.s307-g268: برافروخته is a past/participial adjective 'kindled, blazing' functioning as a predicate or modifier for the following incense items. عنبر و عود خام names the materials: عنبر (amber/ambergris) and عود خام (raw/green agarwood); خام qualifies عود. The verb/adjective bārafrokhteh predicates that these substances were burning or aflame.
segments.s307-g269: تو گفتی که is the reporting clause 'you said that' (تو گفتی = simple past). که introduces the reported content. کیوان is the subject (the planet Saturn as a cosmological actor). ز چرخ is an ablative/locative phrase 'from the sky/heaven' (ز = from, چرخ = sky); برین is the preposition بر + enclitic این ('upon these') preparing the object in the next segment (a typical split of phrase across hemistiches).
segments.s307-g270: ستاره is the (plural) object 'stars'. فشاند is the classical present/habitual/simple verb 'scatters/throws' (third-person singular); همی is the old epic particle marking continuous/habitual or emphatic aspect (often translated as 'was/ever/kept'). بر زمین is the prepositional complement 'onto the earth' (بر = upon, زمین = earth).
segments.s307-g271: مه بانوان is a possessive/metaphorical epithet 'the moon of the ladies' (i.e. the most radiant woman). شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became/came' here meaning 'approached'. به نزدیک تخت is a directional phrase 'to near the throne' (به marks motion/toward + نزدیک 'near' + تخت 'throne'), so the whole segment says she approached the throne.
segments.s307-g272: ابر شهریار is a poetic pairing where ابر ('cloud' literally) functions as an elevated epithet with شهریار ('the king') — reading together as 'the lofty/mighty sovereign'. آفرین کرد is an idiom: آفرین as an exclamation 'bravo' and کرد (did/made) yields 'he exclaimed/praised'. سخت is an adverbial intensifier 'strongly/loudly'. Taken together: 'the mighty king heartily cried “afarin.”'
segments.s307-g361: من (subject) + ایرانیان را (definite object, marked by را) precedes the predicate. یکایک ('each and every') emphasizes that the statement applies to every individual. نه + دیر ... کن(م) is a split negative construction: نه negates the action 'دیر کردن' (to delay/keep waiting), and the main verb is provided later (کنم). Word order is poetic: object and focus adverb precede the verb which appears in the next segment.
segments.s307-g362: کنم is the 1sg present/subjunctive form of کردن used as the main verbal kernel; it connects back to the negated phrase in the previous segment (نه دیر کنم = 'I will not delay'). یکسر ('entirely/utterly') is an adverb modifying the following predicative phrase. از introduces the source (from the treasury), گنج دینار is a genitive/nominal phrase 'treasure (of) dinars', and سیر functions as a predicative adjective/nominal meaning 'sated' (with the implied object being the Iranians). The verb کنم governs both the negated 'دیر' and the following result clause 'یکسر ... سیر'.
segments.s307-g363: ز introduces the reason/source ('from/because of'). خون ریختن is a verbal-noun phrase 'the shedding of blood'. دل is the noun that functions as the thing to be 'taken/avenged' and is placed before the modal. بباید is the impersonal modal 'one must/ought to'; it precedes the main verb (classical word order). کشید is the main verb meaning 'to draw/take/avenge' here (the action required because of bloodshed).
segments.s307-g364: سر بیگناهان ('the heads of the innocent') is the direct object phrase (سر + possessed group بیگناهان). نباید is the negative impersonal modal 'must not', and برید is the main verb 'to cut / to sever' (here 'to behead/cut off'), giving the prohibition. The negative modal + verb expresses a general rule forbidding the action.
segments.s307-g365: نه مردی بود is an existential negation: 'there was no man'. What follows (خیره آشوفتن) is an infinitival/verbal-noun predicate describing the omitted behavior ('to stand gaping/astonished and be thrown into confusion'); بود (past-of-be) links the subject to that predicate in a classical, somewhat compressed word order.
segments.s307-g366: به زیر اندر آورده is a compound locative/participial phrase: به زیر ('to/down beneath') + اندر آورده (the participle 'brought in/brought down'), describing someone 'brought low'. را is the direct-object marker referring back to that person, and کوفتن is the infinitive 'to strike/beat' (used here as the action that such a person would not perform). The phrase thus means 'to strike the one who has been brought down.'
segments.s307-g367: ز introduces the source/agent from whom one should turn. پوشیده‌رویان ('those with veiled/covered faces') is the plural noun phrase. بپیچید is the verbal form of پیچیدن with the بـ prefix and the -ید ending; in context it functions as an instruction/imperative or general counsel 'turn away' (the form can be an imperative or hortative in epic diction). روی is the object 'face' (turn your face). The order is 'from X turn (your) face' which is typical poetic placement.
segments.s307-g368: هر آن کس که is a standard relative expression 'whoever / any person who'. پوشیده دارد literally 'has hiddenness' — here an idiom meaning 'keeps himself concealed/veiled'; دارد is the present-tense verb 'has/keeps'. به کوی is a locative phrase 'in the quarter/alley' (به used as a locative preposition). The whole relative clause qualifies 'هر آن کس' — 'anyone who keeps himself hidden in the street/quarter.'
segments.s307-g321: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning "when." بشنید is a simple past (preterite) third-person singular "he heard;" in classical Persian the verb commonly precedes its subject, so خسرو follows the verb as its subject. ببخشود is a past-tense verb meaning "he gave/granted" (here used transitively without an explicit direct object in the line), and سخت functions adverbially modifying ببخشود (intensifying the giving). The clause overall reads: "When Khosrow heard, he (firmly/strongly) granted."
segments.s307-g322: بر is the preposition "upon/to;" آن is the demonstrative "those/that;" خوبرویان is a plural noun "the fair-/beautiful-faced ones" (here people being praised). برگشته is a perfect/participle meaning "returned/turned (around)" and modifies بخت, which means "fortune/lot." Word order is poetically compact: "upon those fair-faced ones (for whom) fortune had turned/returned."
segments.s307-g323: که is a subordinating/relative particle linking this clause to the previous one ("that/for"). پوشیده‌رویان is a descriptive plural "those with covered/veiled faces" (i.e., modest women). از آن indicates cause "from/because of that," and درد و داغ is a compound meaning emotional suffering: درد = sorrow/pain and داغ = burning grief. Together they give the causal reason for the change in appearance.
segments.s307-g324: شده is the past participle/compound-past of شدن meaning "have become." لعل is used adjectivally here as "ruby-/deep-red," describing رخسارشان (رخسار + ـشان = "their cheek/face"). چون introduces a simile "like/as," and چراغ is the comparison term "lamp/light." The construction states the result: "their cheeks became ruby, like lamps."
segments.s307-g325: بپیچید is the past tense of پیچیدن (here used impersonally/intransitively) meaning "were twisted/was wrung." دل is the direct object "heart(s)." بخردان (بخرد + ان) is the plural "the wise (ones)" and را is the definite object marker attached after بخردان, so the phrase means "the hearts of the wise" (literally: "it twisted the hearts of the wise"). ز درد gives the cause "from/because of pain." Note the poetic word order where the verb precedes the object phrase.
segments.s307-g326: ز here (= از) again marks cause/relatedness "because of/for." فرزند و زن are the nouns "child and wife" (زن is here best read as "wife"). هر کسی is the subject phrase "each person/everyone," and یاد کرد is a periphrastic past meaning "remembered/lamented" (یاد + کرد = to bring to mind/remember). The clause reads: "each person remembered his child and his wife."
segments.s307-g327: همی is an iterative/adverbial particle meaning "continually/kept on." خواندند is past-tense third-person plural "they called/sang/cried out;" here it means they loudly voiced or cheered. آفرینی is an interjection/exclamation ("afarini" = "bravo/well done") and بزرگ qualifies it as "great/loud." The phrase means "they continuously shouted a great 'bravo.'"
segments.s307-g328: سران is the plural "leaders/heads." سپه is a genitival modifier meaning "of the army/host," so سران سپه = "the leaders of the host/army." مهتران is another plural epithet for "chiefs/elders," placed appositionally, and سترگ (an archaic/poetic adjective) here qualifies them as "great/prominent." The whole string is a stacked apposition: "the leaders of the host, the eminent/great chiefs." (Note: سترگ is an archaic adjective meaning "great" in context.)
segments.s307-g345: The clause places the object 'تن خویش را' (his own body) before the verb in the typical Persian object–verb order; 'کسی' is the subject placed at the end for emphasis. 'بد نخواهد' is a negative modal construction with نخواهد (from خواستن) meaning 'will wish (ill) not' → 'will not wish ill'; together: 'No one will wish his body ill.'
segments.s307-g346: چو functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. خواهد is the future/auxiliary sense 'will', and زمانش 'his time' is the topic of the subordinate clause. نباشد is a negative subjunctive/optative of بودن meaning 'there will not be / is not' (here 'is not sufficient'), and بسی 'enough'. Together the clause means 'when his time is not sufficient'.
segments.s307-g347: بباشید is the plural/formal imperative of بودن 'be (you)'; ایمن is a predicative/adverbial adjective 'safe'. The preposition به + محل ('ایوان خویش') marks location: 'be safe in your own iwan/hall.' The possessive is expressed as خوی + ش (خویش) = 'your/your own'.
segments.s307-g348: به یزدان سپرده is a perfect-participial phrase meaning 'entrusted to God' (سپرده as past participle). The phrase that follows, 'تن و جان خویش', is the object (body and life/soul, with خوی + ش = 'your/one's own'). Word order is indirect object ('to God') + participle + direct object.
segments.s307-g349: به ایرانیان marks the indirect object 'to the Iranians'. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (placed after the indirect object in this word order). پیروزبخت functions as the addressed wish/predication 'be fortunate' or 'fortunate ones' — here an imperative/benediction directed at the addressees.
segments.s307-g350: بماناد is an optative/subjunctive form meaning 'may (it) remain' (archaic/poetic). تا here introduces the scope/result 'so that / until', and جاودان modifies the following nouns as 'eternal'. تاج و تخت are the nouns 'crown and throne' that are wished to remain. Overall: 'May crown and throne remain eternally.'
segments.s307-g351: همه شهر توران is the noun phrase 'all the cities of Turan' (همه quantifies شهر). گرفته is a past participle/past passive meaning 'seized/taken', and به دست is an idiomatic agent/instrument phrase 'into the hand' → 'taken into (someone's) hand' meaning 'captured/under control'. Word order is topicalized noun phrase followed by the participle and the phrase indicating manner/agent.
segments.s307-g352: This short clause is elliptical: به ایران ('in/for Iran') sets the location/beneficiary, شما را marks 'you' as the affected party, and 'سرای و نشست' are two nouns 'dwelling/house and seat/place' supplied as what you have (or are given). The verb is omitted but understood (a common poetic compression), so the literal assembly reads 'to Iran, you — dwelling and seat.'
segments.s307-g337: که is a relative/conjunction 'who/that' introducing the clause; بد کرد is a simple past 'did bad/acted badly' (کرد = did). با + NP expresses the target/recipient of the ill (با here functions like 'to/against'); پرهنر is an adjective placed before مادر forming 'پرهنرِ مادر' = 'my cultured/noble mother'; the final م is the possessive clitic attached to مادر (here shown separately in the tokenization).
segments.s307-g338: کسی را is the (indefinite) person marked with the accusative/ topical marker را (here 'anyone' as the potential recipient). همان بد = 'that same evil' (همان = demonstrative modifying بد). به سر is an idiom 'upon the head' (= to afflict someone); ناورم is a poetic/contracted negative-subjunctive/optative form meaning 'I do not / I will not bring' (compare نیاورم), so the clause says 'I will not bring that same evil upon anyone's head.'
segments.s307-g339: بفرمودشان is the past verb 'he commanded/ordered' (بفرمود) with the clitic شان 'them' (to them). بازگشتن is the infinitive/gerund 'to return' functioning as the verbal complement of the order. به جای = 'to the place / to the position' (به + جای).
segments.s307-g340: چنان = 'such/so' modifying پاک‌زاده, so چنان پاک‌زاده = 'that/so noble-born one'. جهان here qualifies the following title (کدخدای), yielding the hyperbolic epithet 'کدخدایِ جهان' = 'headman/chief of the world'; the whole phrase is appositive/attributive to the place to which they are to return.
segments.s307-g341: بدیشان = 'to them' (بـ + ایشان or بدیشان as a fused form) indicating the indirect object. چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین = thus/so; گفت = said). کایمن شوید is an exhortation: شوید is the jussive/subjunctive 'become' used as 'be' (plural), and کایمن is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'secure/safe' (vocative/exhortative formation), so the phrase functions as 'Be safe' / 'Stay secure.'
segments.s307-g342: ز = 'from/of' (poetic preposition). گوینده گفتار بد = a noun phrase 'the speaker of bad speech' (گوینده = speaker; گفتار = utterance/speech; بد = bad, qualifying گفتار). مشنوید is a polite/plural negative imperative/present-subjunctive 'do not hear/listen' (م- prefix + شنوید), so the clause means 'do not listen to a speaker of bad words.'
segments.s307-g343: کز این پس = 'from now on / henceforth' (کز = 'from/that from which', این پس = 'this after' = 'after this'). شما را ز من بیم نیست is an idiomatic existential construction: 'for you there is no fear from me' = 'you need not fear me' (شما را = topical marker/emphatic object; ز من = 'of/from me'; بیم نیست = 'there is no fear').
segments.s307-g344: مرا here (tokenized as م + را) is the patient/topic marker 'in me' / 'about me' in this existential negation; بی‌وفایی و دژخیم are two nouns joined by و meaning 'infidelity/betrayal and cruelty/oppression'; نیست is the negative copula 'is not' — together: 'There is no treachery nor cruelty in me' (= 'I am not faithless or cruel'). Note this word order (object/topic before نیست) is typical of Persian existential/negation in poetic diction.
segments.s307-g233: چرا چون here is a rhetorical temporal/conditional formula: 'چرا چون ...' = 'why, when/as ...'. پلنگان is the plural noun 'leopards' (subject or simile). به + چنگال تیز expresses instrument/way: 'with/into sharp claws' (به used like English 'with' or 'by'). Word order is poetic: simile phrase placed up front.
segments.s307-g234: نه انگیزد is a negative verb form: نه + انگیزد = 'should not/would not arouse' (3rd sing., here with a hortative/optative flavour used in narrative). از خان او رستخیز is the object phrase 'an uprising from his khan/house' (از = 'from', خان = 'inn/house' here, او = 'his', رستخیز = 'uprising').
segments.s307-g235: فرود آورد is a compound past action 'brought down' (فرود + آورد, literal 'fell/brought'). کاخ و ایوان اوی is the definite object 'his palace and its iwan' — اوی is the possessive 'his/her' attached after ایوان, and و links the two nouns.
segments.s307-g236: برانگیزد here functions as 'raised/kindled' (he caused to rise). آتش ز کیوان اوی = 'a fire from Kiwan of his' (ز = 'from' in poetic register; کیوان refers to the planet/name Kiwan; اوی = 'his'). The verb is narrative (historic) and connects the second action to the first.
segments.s307-g237: ز گفتار ایرانیان is a prepositional phrase 'from the speech/words of the Iranians' (ز = 'from/of' in poetry). پس خبر places the time/sequence: 'then/news' — i.e. 'then news (followed)'. گفتار = 'speech', خبر = 'news'.
segments.s307-g238: خبر به کیخسرو آمد = 'news came to Kay Khosrow' (به = 'to', آمد = past 'came'). همه در به در is an idiomatic phrase 'everyone door-to-door' meaning they were roaming or driven from house to house; here در = 'door' (not 'in'). The second به functions as the linker in the idiom 'در به در' ('door to door').
segments.s307-g239: فرستاد کس = 'he sent someone' (فرستاد past 'sent', کس = 'someone'). بخردان را بخواند is a purpose clause with the subjunctive/infinitival flavour: 'to call/summon the wise ones' — را is the definite object marker attached to بخردان, and بخواند here means 'to call/summon' in this construction.
segments.s307-g240: بسی داستان = 'many/much story' (بسی used as a quantifier 'much/many'); پیش ایشان = 'before them / in their presence' (پیش = 'in the presence of'); براند is a narrative verb 'he/they set forth/caused to be related' (here 'recount, present' in the narrative sense). Word order places the quantity + object before the prepositional phrase.
segments.s307-g289: چنان is an adverb meaning “thus/so” placed before the verb; کرد is the simple past “did/acted.” بدگوهر is an epithet (literally “bad‑gem,” here “ill‑natured/evil‑hearted”) that directly precedes the proper name افراسیاب, so the whole phrase reads “Thus acted Afrasiab the ill‑natured.”
segments.s307-g290: که introduces a relative/result clause “who/so that.” پیش تو = “in your presence / before you” (پیش + personal pronoun تو). پوزش is the noun “pardon/forgiveness.” نبیند is a negative form of دیدن (ن + بیند) used here as a jussive/presentive third‑person (“does not / would not see/allow”); in epic Persian this form often reports past behaviour. به خواب is a manner/setting phrase “in (his) sleep / even in his dreams.”
segments.s307-g291: بسی here means “much/a great deal.” دادمش is the verb داد + the enclitic‑object (ـمش) meaning “I gave him/it.” پند = “advice/counsel.” و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” سودی نداشت is a negated past construction: سودی “any benefit” + نداشت (past third‑person of داشتن) = “it had no benefit / it was of no use.”
segments.s307-g292: به خیره is a manner phrase: به + خیره (here “with a fixed stare / in a daze”); همی is the habitual/continuous particle used in epic narrative (“still/continually”). سر ز پندم بگاشت is an idiom: سر = “head,” ز = “from/of,” پندم = “my advice” (ـم = my), and بگاشت is a past form of گذاشتن (here “he set/put” used idiomatically = “he turned away / opposed”). Together: “he remained staring and still turned his head away from my advice (i.e. he ignored my counsel).”
segments.s307-g293: گوای here functions as “witness” (literally ‘testifier’). منست is an archaic/copular phrase element meaning roughly “is/stands (for me)” or “is mine/attests” — the line is an oath formula — and آفریننده‌ام is “my Creator” (آفریننده + possessive suffix ـام ‘my’). Together the phrase reads “My Creator is witness” (an oath: the Creator bears witness).
segments.s307-g294: که introduces the content of the oath: “that …” بارید is a past intransitive verb “fell/rained” used metaphorically for tears/blood. خون = “blood.” از marks source “from/out of.” دو بیننده‌ام literally “my two beholders” — a poetic circumlocution for “my two eyes” (بیننده here = ‘beholder/seer’ with the possessive ـم). The whole clause: “that blood rained from my two eyes.”
segments.s307-g295: چو is the comparative/simile particle “like/as.” گرسیوز و جهن are proper names joined by و (“and”). پیوند تو = پیوند (“bond/kinship/link”) + separate possessive pronoun تو (“your”), so the phrase reads “like Garsivaz and Jehan, your kinsmen/your bond.” The owner pronoun follows the noun in this older style.
segments.s307-g296: که introduces a relative clause “who/which.” ساید here is an archaic/poetic verbal/adjectival form meaning something like “is at work/acts” or “prevails/affects” (contextually “who now do something to …”). به is the preposition marking manner or instrument (“by/with”), زاری = “lamentation/wailing,” کنون = “now,” بند = “bond/fetter,” and تو = “your.” The clause therefore reads “who now, by their lamentation, (do something to) your bond” — i.e. “who now, by wailing, loosen or rend your bonds.”

sentences.s307-s197: From every side, envoys bound for the king set out on the road one by one.
sentences.s307-s198: With gifts and letters from the chiefs, they became the king's attendants one by one.
sentences.s307-s149: Because of Siavash, who is in my khan, what cruel treatment has befallen my heart and life.
sentences.s307-s150: Afrasiab, that ill‑minded man, heard much counsel, but it did him no good.
sentences.s307-s151: Know this: when such a day comes to pass, his kingship will be turned upside down.
sentences.s307-s152: His hat and girdle were given to plunder; his day has become ruined and his head turned back.
sentences.s307-s129: A lamenting cry rose from the iwan: "O just king, be very mindful."
sentences.s307-s130: You know that we are sorely wretched; we are not meant for a life of humiliation nor to be a public warning.
sentences.s307-s131: The head of the women came before the king; the young maidens entered, though reluctant.
sentences.s307-s132: Before each maiden stood a hundred admirers; on every head was a diadem of ruby.
sentences.s307-s21: Two hundred wheeled machines at every gate, armed with bows; from the fortress wall the top peered like a wary head.
sentences.s307-s22: A trebuchet appeared beside it; like dew it kept striking upon its head.
